key_verses.title: Renunciation Alone Does Not Liberate
key_verses.sanskrit:
न कर्मणामनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यं पुरुषोऽश्नुते ।
न च संन्यसनादेव सिद्धिं समधिगच्छति ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
na karmaṇāmanārambhānnaiṣkarmyaṁ puruṣo’śnute ।
na ca sannyasanādeva siddhiṁ samadhigacchati ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: A person does not attain liberation by merely avoiding action, nor does one attain liberation by mere external renunciation.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now begins to show why Arjuna should not think of escaping from action. Arjuna imagines that if he avoids karma, he may move toward jñānam and mokṣa. This shloka directly removes that misunderstanding.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now begins to show why Arjuna should not think of escaping from action. Arjuna imagines that if he avoids karma, he may move toward jñānam and mokṣa. This shloka directly removes that misunderstanding.

Avoiding action does not give naiṣkarmyam. Here naiṣkarmyam means mokṣa, freedom, or peace of mind. If the problem of saṁsāra were caused merely by activity, then stopping activity would solve the problem. But the real problem is ignorance. An ignorant mind will carry saṁsāra wherever it goes. It may leave the battlefield, family, office, or society, but it cannot leave itself. Therefore, physical withdrawal from duty cannot by itself give freedom.

The second half of the shloka adds another important point. Mere sannyāsa does not give siddhi, which here also means mokṣa. External renunciation — wearing ochre robes, living in an āśrama, going to the Himalayas, giving up possessions — does not automatically remove ignorance. Renunciation becomes spiritually meaningful only when it is supported by Vedānta-vijñāna, clear knowledge born of scriptural study and understanding.

Therefore, whether one lives as a grihastha or as a sannyāsī, two things are required: preparation of the mind and knowledge. Preparation requires karma-yoga. Liberation requires jñāna-yoga. Sannyāsa without knowledge does not guarantee freedom; actionlessness without inner maturity does not guarantee peace. For Arjuna, the conclusion is clear: do not run away from the required action. Perform karma-yoga, purify the mind, gain knowledge, and be free.
key_verses.story:
Bharata and the Deer
King Bharata had ruled well and lived a life of responsibility. Later, he renounced the kingdom and went to the forest for spiritual pursuit. Outwardly, everything looked ideal. He had left power, wealth, family responsibilities, and royal comforts. He lived in a quiet hermitage, engaged in worship and contemplation.
One day, while he was near the river, he saw a pregnant deer frightened by the roar of a lion. In terror, the deer leapt across the river, gave birth, and died. The newborn fawn was left helpless. Bharata’s heart melted. He took the little deer to his āśrama and cared for it.
At first, the care seemed compassionate and harmless. He fed the fawn, protected it, and watched it grow. Slowly, however, his mind became attached. During prayer, he wondered where the deer was. During meditation, he worried whether it had eaten. If the deer wandered away, he became restless. The forest had become quiet, but his mind was no longer quiet.
At the time of death, Bharata’s mind was absorbed in the deer. Because of that attachment, he was born as a deer in the next life. His external renunciation had been great, but a subtle inner attachment remained. Later, through further maturity, he was born as Jaḍa Bharata and attained wisdom.
This story powerfully illustrates the teaching of 3.4. Bharata had given up royal action and had physically withdrawn from society, but mere withdrawal did not by itself produce liberation. The mind must be purified and ignorance must be removed. Without inner freedom, even a forest can become another saṁsāra. Therefore, naiṣkarmyam does not come by merely avoiding karma, and siddhi does not come by mere external renunciation.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka is very relevant today because we often imagine that peace will come by changing the outer situation. We think, “If I leave this job, I will be peaceful. If I move away from my family pressures, I will be spiritual. If I go to an āśrama, all my problems will disappear. If I stop doing everything, my mind will become quiet.”

Sometimes an outer change may be necessary. But this shloka warns us that outer change alone does not solve the inner problem. The restless mind goes with us. The hurt mind goes with us. The ego goes with us. Likes, dislikes, expectations, fear, anger, and insecurity travel with us. If they are not purified through karma-yoga and resolved through knowledge, they will reappear in a new place, with new people, and under a new name.

In Indian family life, this is especially important. A person may feel burdened by parents, spouse, children, work, rituals, society, or financial duties. The mind may say, “I want to leave everything and become spiritual.” But the question is: is this renunciation born of maturity, or is it born of fatigue and avoidance? If it is avoidance, it will not give freedom.

The verse also protects us from superficial spirituality. Spiritual life is not a costume, location, title, or lifestyle image. A person may sit in an āśrama and remain full of attachment. Another may live in a house, perform duties, serve, study, and grow steadily. What matters is not merely where we are, but whether the mind is being purified and whether knowledge is being pursued.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I trying to escape an outer situation without addressing the inner cause of my disturbance?
Do I secretly believe that a change of place, role, or appearance will automatically make me spiritual?
What karma-yoga is available in my present life that I may be trying to avoid?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa tells Arjuna that we do not become peaceful just by avoiding work. If a child does not do homework, the homework does not disappear. The worry remains. If someone says, “I will not clean my room,” the room does not become clean by itself.
Running away from a job is not the same as finishing it properly. Also, wearing a special dress or sitting in a quiet place does not automatically make the mind wise. The mind becomes better when we do the right things, learn properly, and understand clearly.
So Arjuna should not think, “If I avoid this difficult duty, I will become free.” He has to do what is right, make his mind strong and clean, and then gain wisdom.
kids_content.story:
The Unfinished Room
One Saturday morning, Ananya’s mother said, “Ananya, please clean your room before you go out to play.”
Ananya looked at the room. Her books were on the floor, crayons were under the bed, clothes were on the chair, and puzzle pieces were everywhere. It looked like a tiny storm had visited.
She sighed. “This is too much work.”
Then she had an idea. She took a bedsheet and spread it over the mess. The floor looked a little better. Then she closed the room door and went to the living room with a big smile.
“Amma, I am done.”
Her mother came and opened the room. At first, the room looked quiet. But one corner of the bedsheet was sticking up. When her mother lifted it, all the books, crayons, clothes, and puzzle pieces were still there.
Ananya looked down.
Her mother said gently, “Closing the door does not clean the room. Covering the mess does not remove the mess. We have to put each thing in its place.”
So Ananya began. Books went to the shelf. Crayons went into the box. Clothes went into the basket. Puzzle pieces went into the tin. It took time. It was not fun at first. But when the room was truly clean, Ananya felt happy.
Later, when she went out to play, her mind was light. She did not have to worry that the mess was waiting under the sheet.
Arjuna’s mind also has a kind of “room” to clean. If he simply runs away from duty, the inner mess will remain. Karma-yoga is like cleaning the room properly. Then knowledge can make the mind truly free.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one job I try to hide from or delay instead of doing properly?
When I finish a duty well, does my mind feel lighter?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Avoiding action
Phrase: na karmaṇām anārambhāt = not by non-beginning / avoidance of actions The verse begins by rejecting the idea that freedom comes by avoiding karma. Anārambha means not undertaking action. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says that mere non-performance of action does not produce liberation.
Step 2: The seeker does not gain naiṣkarmyam this way
Phrase: naiṣkarmyaṁ puruṣo na aśnute = a person does not attain naiṣkarmyam Here naiṣkarmyam does not merely mean physical actionlessness. It means mokṣa, freedom from bondage, or peace. A person does not become free simply by stopping activity because bondage is caused by ignorance, not by action alone.
Step 3: The hidden assumption being corrected
Phrase: na karmaṇām anārambhāt naiṣkarmyam = not liberation through avoidance of action The verse corrects Arjuna’s possible assumption: “If karma is troublesome, I can avoid karma and gain peace.” That is not true. If the mind is ignorant and unprepared, inaction can leave the same sorrow, fear, attachment, and confusion intact.
Step 4: Mere renunciation is also insufficient
Phrase: na ca sannyasanāt eva = nor by mere renunciation alone The word eva is important. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa is not rejecting sannyāsa itself. He is rejecting mere sannyāsa. External renunciation by itself does not give liberation.
Step 5: What “mere sannyāsa” means
Phrase: sannyasanāt eva = by renunciation alone Mere sannyāsa means external changes without knowledge: wearing ochre robes, living in an āśrama, going to the Himalayas, giving up household possessions, or changing one’s social identity. These may have value when joined with knowledge and maturity, but by themselves they do not remove ignorance.
Step 6: Siddhi means mokṣa here
Phrase: siddhiṁ samadhigacchati = one attains accomplishment / liberation Here siddhi means mokṣa, the same as naiṣkarmyam in the first line. The two halves of the verse make one teaching: neither action-avoidance nor mere external renunciation gives liberation.
Step 7: The proper requirement
Phrase: whole shloka Whether one lives in grihastha-āśrama or sannyāsa-āśrama, purification and knowledge are necessary. Karma-yoga prepares the mind. Jñāna-yoga removes ignorance. Therefore, Arjuna should not run away from duty. He should use karma as preparation and later gain knowledge.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Saubhari Ṛṣi and the Hidden Mind
Saubhari Ṛṣi wanted to live away from disturbance. He chose an unusual place for tapas: he sat underwater in deep meditation. There were no village sounds, no household duties, no social distractions, no crowds, no kingdom, no marketplace. Outwardly, it looked like complete withdrawal.
But one day, while seated underwater, he saw fish moving together. He observed their family life and companionship. A desire arose in his mind. The outer world had been avoided, but the inner seed of longing had not been removed.
That desire grew. Saubhari came out of the water and approached King Māndhātā, asking to marry one of his daughters. By yogic power, he assumed a beautiful form, and the princesses chose him. He entered household life, enjoyed pleasures, and lived surrounded by family and luxury.
After a long time, he recognized the power of the mind and the limitation of external withdrawal. Merely sitting away from society had not destroyed desire. The unprepared mind carried saṁsāra even underwater.
This story directly illustrates 3.4. One may avoid ordinary actions and live in a remote place, but an unpurified mind can still be bound. Naiṣkarmyam does not come from merely avoiding action. Inner preparation and knowledge are necessary.

2. Viśvāmitra’s Tapas and the Return of Desire
Viśvāmitra was a mighty king who later turned toward tapas. He left royal life and performed intense austerities. His determination was extraordinary. He stood in forests, endured heat and cold, and gathered great spiritual power.
Yet the journey was not complete merely because he had left the kingdom. Again and again, inner impurities surfaced. Anger, pride, rivalry, and desire appeared in different forms. The episode of Menakā is especially well known. Menakā came to disturb his tapas, and Viśvāmitra was drawn into desire. His austerity was interrupted.
This does not diminish his greatness. In fact, his story is powerful because it shows the long struggle required for inner mastery. He did not become a Brahmarṣi merely by leaving the throne. External renunciation was only one part. The mind had to be purified, humbled, steadied, and illumined.
This connects to the shloka because sannyāsa or withdrawal alone does not guarantee siddhi. If anger, pride, and desire remain, the forest becomes another field of struggle. Real freedom requires inner transformation and knowledge.

3. The Monk and the Woman at the River
Two monks were walking along a muddy riverbank after heavy rain. The river was swollen, and the path had become slippery. Near the crossing stood a young woman, afraid to step into the current.
One monk immediately lifted her, carried her across the river, placed her safely on the other side, and continued walking. The second monk was disturbed. He said nothing for a long time, but his mind kept repeating, “How could he touch a woman? We are monks. This is improper.”
After several miles, he could no longer remain silent. He scolded the first monk: “You carried that woman across the river!”
The first monk smiled and said, “I left her at the river. Are you still carrying her?”
The story shows the difference between outer renunciation and inner freedom. One monk performed an action without attachment and moved on. The other externally avoided the action, but mentally carried the woman for miles.
This story illustrates 3.4 in a simple way. Mere external restraint or renunciation is not freedom. The real question is whether the mind is free. Avoiding action while mentally holding on does not give naiṣkarmyam.

4. Śikhidhvaja Leaves the Kingdom but Carries Ignorance
King Śikhidhvaja, described in the Yoga Vāsiṣṭha tradition, became convinced that worldly life was the obstacle to liberation. He left his kingdom, his palace, and his wife Cūḍālā, and went to the forest. There he lived austerely, performed tapas, and considered himself a renunciate.
But Cūḍālā, who had gained true wisdom, knew that her husband had only changed location. His ignorance had not ended. He had renounced the kingdom outwardly, but inwardly he still held notions of “I am a renunciate,” “I am practicing,” “I am progressing.” The ego had simply taken a spiritual form.
Cūḍālā approached him in disguise as a young sage and taught him. Through that teaching, Śikhidhvaja gradually understood that freedom is not produced by merely leaving the palace or sitting in the forest. Bondage is ignorance; freedom is knowledge.
This story strongly reflects the verse. Sannyasanāt eva — by mere renunciation alone — siddhi is not attained. Śikhidhvaja needed knowledge, not only forest life. Without understanding, renunciation can become another identity.

5. The Four Kumāras and Mature Renunciation
The four Kumāras — Sanaka, Sanandana, Sanātana, and Sanatkumāra — were born from Brahmā. Brahmā wanted them to participate in creation. But they were inwardly mature, detached, and committed to knowledge. They chose the path of renunciation from the very beginning.
Their renunciation was not escapism. They were not avoiding responsibility out of fear, fatigue, or dislike. They had no worldly craving to fulfill and no need to prove themselves through action. Their minds were already turned toward Brahman. They wandered as knowers and teachers, established in spiritual pursuit.
This story helps us understand the nuance of 3.4. The shloka does not condemn true renunciation. It rejects mere renunciation. For rare prepared minds, renunciation may be natural and meaningful. But for an unprepared mind, imitating such renunciation will not give mokṣa.
The Kumāras show genuine nivṛtti; Arjuna’s proposed withdrawal is not of that kind. Therefore Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa directs him toward karma-yoga first.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: न कर्मणाम् अनारम्भात् नैष्कर्म्यम् पुरुषः अश्नुते । न च संन्यसनात् एव सिद्धिम् समधिगच्छति ॥३.४॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: न = not | कर्मणाम् = of actions | अनारम्भात् = from non-commencement, from avoidance | नैष्कर्म्यम् = liberation, freedom from bondage | पुरुषः = a person | अश्नुते = attains | न = not | च = and | संन्यसनात् = from renunciation | एव = merely, alone | सिद्धिम् = liberation, accomplishment | समधिगच्छति = attains fully
rich_grammar.anvayah: पुरुषः कर्मणाम् अनारम्भात् नैष्कर्म्यं न अश्नुते । संन्यसनात् एव च सिद्धिं न समधिगच्छति ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: कर्मणाम् + अनारम्भात् = कर्मणामनारम्भात् | अनारम्भात् + नैष्कर्म्यम् = अनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यम् | पुरुषः + अश्नुते = पुरुषोऽश्नुते | संन्यसनात् + एव = संन्यसनादेव | सिद्धिम् + समधिगच्छति = सिद्धिं
rich_grammar.samasa: अनारम्भः = न आरम्भः Samāsa name: नञ्-तत्पुरुष समासः; non-commencement, non-undertaking. | नैष्कर्म्यम् is a derived noun meaning the state of freedom from karma/bondage. It is not necessary to force a separate samāsa analysis here.
rich_grammar.other: अनारम्भात् and संन्यसनात् are पञ्चमी विभक्ति forms expressing “from” or “because of.” | एव emphasizes “mere” or “alone,” and is central to the teaching: mere renunciation is not enough. | अश्नुते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, meaning “attains” or “reaches.” | समधिगच्छति is लट्-लकारः, परस्मैपदम्, meaning “attains fully” or “reaches completely.” | नैष्कर्म्यम् and सिद्धिम् are both used here in the sense of mokṣa.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Liberation is not achieved by merely stopping action or by wearing the external marks of renunciation. What matters is inner transformation — the purification of the mind through disciplined, selfless action performed with the right attitude.

key_verses.title: No One Can Remain Without Action
key_verses.sanskrit:
न हि कश्चित्क्षणमपि जातु तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत् ।
कार्यते ह्यवशः(ख्) कर्म सर्वः(फ्) प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
na hi kaścitkṣaṇamapi jātu tiṣṭhatyakarmakṛt ।
kāryate hyavaśaḥ(kh) karma sarvaḥ(f) prakṛtijairguṇaiḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: No one can remain without action even for a moment, because everyone is helplessly made to act by the guṇas born of prakṛti.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa gives the second reason why Arjuna should not think of escaping action. In the previous shloka, he showed that giving up action does not give mokṣa. Now he says that giving up action is not even possible.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa gives the second reason why Arjuna should not think of escaping action. In the previous shloka, he showed that giving up action does not give mokṣa. Now he says that giving up action is not even possible.

No person can remain actionless even for a moment. One may stop physical movement, but the mind may become more active. One may sit quietly, but thoughts run everywhere. One may give up outer duties, but worry, memory, planning, imagination, desire, fear, and regret continue. Even when the body is still, some form of activity continues at the level of body, speech, mind, or subtle tendencies.

Why does this happen? Because every person is governed by prakṛti through the three guṇas — sattva, rajas, and tamas. These guṇas shape one’s svabhāva, one’s inner disposition. According to that disposition, a person is pushed into some form of action. A rajasic person will seek activity, projects, argument, achievement, leadership, or restlessness. A tamasic person may seek sleep, avoidance, dullness, or carelessness. A sattvic person may seek study, prayer, service, contemplation, or order. But some movement will be there.

Therefore, the aim of spiritual life is not to forcibly stop all action. That is unrealistic for an unprepared mind. The aim is to redirect action. The energy of the mind and body must be channelized into karma-yoga. Just as flowing water should not be wasted or violently blocked, human energy should be guided into dhārmic action, service, prayer, discipline, and preparation for knowledge.

For Arjuna, the message is direct: “Do not imagine that dropping the bow will make you actionless. Your nature will push you into some action anyway. Therefore, do not run away from karma. Convert the action that has come to you into karma-yoga.”
key_verses.story:
Ratnākara’s Restless Energy Becomes Vālmīki’s Tapas
Ratnākara lived in the forest as a robber. His life was full of action, but it was action driven by ignorance, fear, and attachment. He stopped travelers, threatened them, and took from them. He believed he was doing it for his family. His body was active, his mind was alert, but his action was not guided by dharma.
One day, he stopped the sage Nārada. But Nārada was not afraid. He looked at Ratnākara with compassion and asked him a question: “You say you commit these actions for your family. Will they share the pāpa of these actions?”
Ratnākara was certain they would. He tied Nārada to a tree and went home to ask. But his family answered honestly. They would share the food and comfort he brought, but not the pāpa of his actions. Ratnākara was shaken. The action he had justified for years suddenly stood before him in a new light.
He returned to Nārada, no longer proud. He wanted to change. Nārada did not tell him, “Simply stop all action and become free.” Ratnākara’s mind was not ready for quiet knowledge. His energy had to be redirected. Nārada gave him a discipline. Since Ratnākara could not even chant “Rāma” properly, he was asked to repeat “mara, mara,” which gradually became “Rāma, Rāma.”
Ratnākara sat in tapas. The same intensity that once went into robbery now went into spiritual discipline. Ants built an anthill around him. Time passed. The restless forest robber became the sage Vālmīki, the seer of the Rāmāyaṇa.
This story illustrates the teaching of 3.5. Human beings cannot simply erase action. Their guṇas and svabhāva will express themselves. The spiritual solution is not suppression but redirection. Ratnākara’s energy was not destroyed; it was purified and channelized. That is karma-yoga’s role: to transform activity into preparation for wisdom.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka is extremely practical. Many people say, “I want to do nothing. I want to quit everything. I want to sit quietly.” But when they actually sit quietly, the mind does not become quiet. It starts planning, remembering, worrying, scrolling, comparing, fantasizing, judging, or complaining.

This is visible in daily Indian life. A person says, “I want one peaceful Sunday,” but by morning he is checking messages, planning visits, watching videos, rearranging things, getting irritated, or looking for entertainment. A student says, “I will just relax,” but the mind runs toward the phone. A retired person says, “Now I have no duties,” but new worries and projects appear. A spiritual seeker says, “I will meditate,” but the mind becomes louder than before.

The teaching is not pessimistic. It does not say, “You are helpless, so do anything.” It says, “Understand the power of prakṛti and guṇas. Then intelligently redirect them.” A rajasic person can serve, organize, study, work honestly, exercise discipline, and contribute. A tamasic tendency can be countered by routine, prayer, movement, cleanliness, and responsibility. A sattvic tendency can be nourished through śāstra, japa, worship, reflection, and compassion.

In family life, work life, and spiritual life, the question is not “How do I avoid all action?” The better question is, “How do I make my unavoidable action purifying?” Karma-yoga begins when we stop fighting the fact of action and start sanctifying it.
key_verses.reflection:
When I try to be inactive, what kind of activity does my mind immediately run toward?
Which guṇa seems to dominate me often — sattva, rajas, or tamas — and how does it shape my actions?
How can I redirect my natural energy into karma-yoga instead of letting it become distraction, restlessness, or avoidance?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa tells Arjuna, “Nobody can stay completely without doing anything.”
Even when we sit still, something is still happening. Our eyes may look around. Our fingers may start playing. Our legs may shake. Our mind may think about games, snacks, friends, homework, or many other things.
So Kṛṣṇa is teaching Arjuna: “Do not think that by dropping your bow, everything will become peaceful. Your mind will still keep moving.”
The better idea is to use our energy in a good way. If we feel active, we can help at home, study, clean our things, pray, or do something useful. Energy is not bad. We just have to guide it properly.
Arjuna also has energy and strength. Kṛṣṇa wants him to use that strength for the right duty, not run away from it.
kids_content.story:
Vikram Cannot Sit Still
Vikram was eight years old and full of energy. After school, he threw his bag on the sofa, kicked off his shoes, and ran into the kitchen.
“Amma, I am bored!”
His mother said, “You just came home. Sit quietly for ten minutes.”
Vikram sat on the chair. For five seconds, he was still. Then his foot began tapping. Then his fingers began drumming on the table. Then he picked up a spoon and spun it. The spoon fell with a loud clang.
“Vikram,” Amma said, “quietly.”
He folded his hands. Then he started humming. Then he leaned back on the chair. Then he looked at the ceiling fan and asked, “How fast is it moving?” Then he stood up to check.
Amma smiled. “Your body is sitting, but your energy is still running.”
Vikram looked embarrassed. “I cannot sit still.”
His grandfather, who was reading nearby, called him. “Come here. I have a job for you.”
He gave Vikram a small basket. “Put all these books back on the shelf. Then water the tulasī plant. Then arrange your pencils.”
Vikram ran happily. He stacked the books neatly. He watered the plant carefully. He sharpened his pencils and put them in a box. After twenty minutes, he came back smiling.
Grandfather asked, “Now how do you feel?”
“Better,” Vikram said. “My mind is not jumping so much.”
Grandfather said, “That is because energy must be used properly. If you only try to stop it, it becomes mischief. If you guide it, it becomes help.”
Arjuna also has energy. He cannot become peaceful just by refusing action. Kṛṣṇa teaches him to guide his action in the right way.
kids_content.reflection:
When I feel restless, what good job can I do instead of making trouble?
Can I use my energy today to help someone, clean something, study, or pray?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: No one is exempt
Phrase: na hi kaścit = indeed, no one Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa makes a universal statement. No person is an exception. As long as one is under the influence of prakṛti and the guṇas, some form of action continues.
Step 2: Not even for a moment
Phrase: kṣaṇamapi jātu = even for a moment, at any time The impossibility of total inaction is not occasional; it applies always. Even a short moment of complete actionlessness is not available to an unprepared person identified with body and mind.
Step 3: Remaining without action is impossible
Phrase: tiṣṭhatyakarmakṛt = remains as one who does no action Akarmakṛt means one who does not act. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says no one can remain in that condition. Stopping physical work does not mean all action has stopped. Mental activity may continue even more strongly.
Step 4: The helplessness of the individual
Phrase: kāryate hyavaśaḥ karma = one is helplessly made to act The word avaśaḥ is important. It means not fully in command, helplessly pushed. The individual thinks, “I will simply stop,” but one’s own nature pushes one into some activity. This is why mere suppression does not work.
Step 5: Everyone is included
Phrase: sarvaḥ = everyone All people are included: active people, withdrawn people, students, householders, renunciates, beginners, and even those who claim they are doing nothing. Until knowledge and maturity are gained, prakṛti continues to operate.
Step 6: The guṇas born of prakṛti
Phrase: prakṛtijairguṇaiḥ = by the guṇas born of prakṛti Prakṛti is the basic cause of the manifest world, and it consists of the three guṇas: sattva, rajas, and tamas. These guṇas shape the mind and personality. According to their proportion, each person develops a svabhāva and is pushed into corresponding action.
Step 7: The teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse does not teach fatalism. It teaches realism. Since action cannot be avoided, action must be purified. The goal is not to stop all activity but to redirect it into karma-yoga. Arjuna should not escape action; he should perform the action appropriate to him in the right spirit.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Dhruva’s Hurt Becomes Tapas
Dhruva was a young prince. One day, he saw his stepbrother sitting on his father Uttānapāda’s lap. Dhruva also wanted to sit there. But his stepmother Suruci harshly stopped him. She told him that he had no right to the king’s lap because he was not born from her womb. If he wanted that place, he should worship Bhagavān and be born again through her.
Dhruva was deeply wounded. He went to his mother Sunīti, crying. Sunīti did not feed his anger. She gently directed him toward Bhagavān. The child’s mind was burning with hurt and ambition. He could not simply sit quietly and forget. His nature was active, intense, and determined.
Dhruva left for the forest. On the way, Nārada met him. Nārada first tried to soften him, saying he was only a child and should return home. But Dhruva’s resolve was firm. Seeing his intensity, Nārada redirected it. He taught him a mantra and a discipline of worship.
Dhruva performed tapas with extraordinary focus. The same rajasic hurt that could have become revenge became spiritual effort. Finally, Bhagavān Viṣṇu appeared before him. Dhruva’s heart was transformed.
This story shows that energy cannot merely be erased. Dhruva’s mind was pushed into action by his svabhāva. The solution was not suppression but redirection. In the language of 3.5, prakṛti was moving; Nārada guided that movement toward karma-yoga and upāsanā.

2. Gajendra’s Instinctive Struggle Becomes Prayer
Gajendra, the king of elephants, once entered a beautiful lake with his herd. The scene was full of pleasure: cool water, lotus flowers, family, strength, and royal confidence. Suddenly, a crocodile caught his foot.
Gajendra fought with all his might. The elephant’s natural strength expressed itself immediately. He pulled, struggled, roared, and tried to free himself. The crocodile, strong in water, held on. The battle continued for a long time. Gajendra’s herd could not save him.
At first, his action was instinctive: physical struggle born of nature. But slowly his strength failed. When bodily power and pride were exhausted, a deeper memory awakened. Gajendra lifted a lotus with his trunk and prayed to Bhagavān.
Bhagavān Viṣṇu came and saved him.
This story shows the movement from helpless action to surrender. As long as Gajendra relied only on natural strength, prakṛti pushed him into struggle. When that struggle became prayer, action was sanctified. The shloka teaches that action will happen; the spiritual question is whether action remains instinctive or becomes an offering.

3. The Ocean Cannot Stop Its Waves
In the Rāmāyaṇa, when Rāma reached the ocean on the way to Laṅkā, the vast sea lay between him and Sītā. Rāma prayed to the ocean for a passage. For three days, the ocean did not appear. Rāma then became stern and prepared to dry it up with his arrow.
The ocean deity finally appeared and explained his nature. Water has its own law. The ocean cannot simply become hard ground by wish. Its nature is to be deep, fluid, and wave-filled. But it could cooperate. It showed Rāma the way: build a bridge with the help of the vānaras, and the ocean would support it.
The ocean did not stop being ocean. Its nature was not destroyed. Instead, its nature was understood and worked with. The vānaras then acted with enthusiasm, bringing stones and trees, and the bridge to Laṅkā was built.
This story illustrates the teaching of 3.5 at a symbolic level. Prakṛti has its laws. The guṇas move. One cannot merely command nature, “Be inactive.” Wisdom lies in understanding nature and channelizing it toward dharma. The ocean’s movement became part of Rāma’s mission rather than an obstacle.

4. The Vānaras’ Restlessness Serves Rāma
The vānaras in the Rāmāyaṇa were full of movement. They leapt, shouted, searched, climbed, argued, celebrated, and rushed from place to place. Their nature was not quiet like forest sages seated in meditation. They were energetic, restless, and expressive.
When Sītā had to be found, that very restless energy became useful. Sugrīva sent vānaras in all directions. Aṅgada led the southern search. Jāmbavān guided the team with wisdom. Hanumān crossed the ocean. Later, when the bridge to Laṅkā had to be built, the vānaras carried rocks, trees, and mountains with tremendous enthusiasm.
Their nature was not suppressed. It was organized. Their rajas became service. Their movement became devotion. Their strength became Rāma-kārya.
This story connects directly to the teaching of 3.5. People act according to svabhāva shaped by guṇas. The goal is not to make everyone outwardly silent or inactive. The goal is to dedicate each nature to dharma. When energy is offered to Bhagavān, even restlessness becomes karma-yoga.

5. Aśvatthāmā’s Unchecked Guṇas Become Destructive
After the Mahābhārata war, Aśvatthāmā was filled with grief, anger, humiliation, and revenge. His father Droṇa had died. The Kauravas were destroyed. Instead of allowing sorrow to be purified by reflection, his mind was seized by tamas and rajas.
At night, he entered the camp of the sleeping Pāṇḍava forces and committed a terrible act of destruction. His action was not guided by dharma or discrimination. He was driven by inner forces he did not master. Anger became violence. Pain became cruelty. Restlessness became adharma.
This story shows the warning side of 3.5. The guṇas will push a person into action. If they are not disciplined, they can become destructive. Merely having strength, knowledge of weapons, or noble lineage does not save a person whose mind is overpowered by rajas and tamas.
The teaching is clear: action is inevitable, but dhārmic channelization is necessary. Karma-yoga is the way to prevent natural energy from becoming bondage and harm.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: न हि कश्चित् क्षणम् अपि जातु तिष्ठति अकर्मकृत् । कार्यते हि अवशः कर्म सर्वः प्रकृतिजैः गुणैः ॥३.५॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: न = not | हि = indeed, because | कश्चित् = anyone | क्षणम् = for a moment | अपि = even | जातु = at any time, ever | तिष्ठति = remains | अकर्मकृत् = one who does not act | कार्यते = is made to do | हि = indeed, because | अवशः = helpless, not independent | कर्म = action | सर्वः = everyone | प्रकृतिजैः = born of prakṛti | गुणैः = by the guṇas
rich_grammar.anvayah: कश्चित् क्षणम् अपि जातु अकर्मकृत् न तिष्ठति । हि सर्वः अवशः प्रकृतिजैः गुणैः कर्म कार्यते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: कश्चित् + क्षणम् = कश्चित्क्षणम् | क्षणम् + अपि = क्षणमपि | तिष्ठति + अकर्मकृत् = तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत् | कार्यते + हि = कार्यते | हि + अवशः = ह्यवशः | प्रकृतिजैः + गुणैः = प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः
rich_grammar.samasa: अकर्मकृत् = अकर्म करोति इति Samāsa name: उपपद-तत्पुरुष / नञ्-भावयुक्त प्रयोगः; one who does no action. In the verse, Bhagavān says no one can remain as such. | प्रकृतिजैः = प्रकृतेः जातैः Samāsa name: पञ्चमी तत्पुरुष समासः; born from prakṛti.
rich_grammar.other: कार्यते is a causative passive form from कृ with णिच्: “is made to do” or “is caused to perform.” | अवशः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग, agreeing with सर्वः. | कर्म is द्वितीया एकवचनम् नपुंसकलिङ्ग and expresses the action that one is made to perform in the passive-causative construction. | क्षणम् functions adverbially: “for a moment.” | जातु is an indeclinable meaning “ever” or “at any time,” strengthening the universality of the statement. | प्रकृतिजैः गुणैः is तृतीया विभक्ति, indicating the instrument or agency: “by the guṇas born of prakṛti.” | प्रकृतिजैः is formed from प्रकृति + ज, meaning “born of prakṛti.” It qualifies गुणैः.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Total inaction is impossible because the guṇas of prakṛti drive every being to act. Since action is unavoidable, the wise choice is not whether to act, but how to act — with discipline, offering, and inner clarity.

key_verses.title: The Danger of False Renunciation
key_verses.sanskrit:
कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य य आस्ते मनसा स्मरन् ।
इन्द्रियार्थान्विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः(स्) स उच्यते ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
karmendriyāṇi saṁyamya ya āste manasā smaran ।
indriyārthānvimūḍhātmā mithyācāraḥ(s) sa ucyate ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: One who outwardly restrains the organs of action but mentally dwells on sense objects is deluded and is called a hypocrite.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now gives the third reason why Arjuna should not choose premature withdrawal from action. In the previous shloka, he showed that nobody can remain without action because everyone is pushed by prakṛti and the guṇas. Now he shows that forced inaction can become dangerous when the mind is not ready.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now gives the third reason why Arjuna should not choose premature withdrawal from action. In the previous shloka, he showed that nobody can remain without action because everyone is pushed by prakṛti and the guṇas. Now he shows that forced inaction can become dangerous when the mind is not ready.

A person may restrain the karmendriyāṇi, the organs of action. He may stop outward activities. He may withdraw from ordinary duties. He may wear the appearance of renunciation. He may physically avoid sense pleasures because his role or dress does not allow him to pursue them. But if the mind still dwells on indriyārthāḥ, sense objects, then the external withdrawal is not genuine freedom.

This is not transcendence; it is suppression. Vedānta does not teach suppression of desire. It teaches growing out of desire through maturity, understanding, and inner freedom. If a person has not grown out of desires but only blocks their expression, the pressure remains in the mind. The person may appear detached outside, while inside the mind is living in artha-kāma. This creates a double life.

Such a person is called mithyācāraḥ — one whose outer conduct and inner condition do not match. He may look like a renunciate externally, but mentally he is still consuming sense pleasures. He may deceive society, because others may think he is absorbed in dharma and mokṣa. More seriously, he deceives himself.

Therefore, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa warns Arjuna: do not choose inaction or sannyāsa before inner maturity. If the mind is not ready for jñāna-niṣṭhā, withdrawal may produce hypocrisy, mental pressure, or fall. It is safer and healthier to live an active life of karma-yoga, where desires are regulated, duties are performed, and the mind gradually matures.
key_verses.story:
Rāvaṇa Comes as a False Ascetic (Rāmāyaṇa)
In the Rāmāyaṇa, Rāvaṇa’s desire for Sītā had already taken hold of his mind. He did not approach openly as a warrior. He did not come as a king declaring his intention. He came wearing the appearance of holiness.
After Mārīca drew Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa away, Sītā was alone in the āśrama. The forest was quiet. The hermitage, where dharma and simplicity lived, suddenly received a visitor. Rāvaṇa arrived disguised as a mendicant ascetic. His form suggested restraint, learning, and renunciation. A person in such a form was to be honored, not suspected.
Sītā received him with the respect due to a guest. But behind the outer appearance of tapas was a mind burning with kāma. His words slowly revealed his inner corruption. He praised her beauty, spoke inappropriately, and finally revealed his real intention. The ascetic appearance fell away. The inner Rāvaṇa stood exposed.
This is a powerful example of mithyācāra. The outer form declared renunciation; the inner mind was dwelling on sense desire. The dress suggested self-control; the mind was enslaved. Rāvaṇa did not merely deceive Sītā; he showed the danger of outer religious appearance without inner purity.
The shloka warns against exactly this kind of split. If the organs of action are restrained but the mind continues to dwell on sense objects, the person is not free. True spirituality is not a costume, posture, or social role. Inner maturity must match outer conduct.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka is very relevant because hypocrisy often begins subtly. A person may say, “I am detached,” but the mind may constantly think of recognition, comfort, pleasure, comparison, or control. Someone may say, “I do not care about money,” but keep checking status and possessions mentally. Someone may say, “I am spiritual,” but spend the mind’s time in jealousy, fantasy, anger, and judgment.

In Indian life, this can happen in religious settings too. A person may sit in pūjā while mentally planning business profits. Someone may attend satsanga while comparing clothes, status, or who received attention. Someone may post spiritual quotes but internally crave praise. A student may keep the phone away during study but mentally keep thinking about reels and games. A person may fast outwardly but spend the whole day thinking only about the evening feast.

The teaching is not to condemn ourselves harshly. It is to become honest. If the mind still wants certain things, we should not pretend that we have transcended them. We should regulate them through dharma, perform karma-yoga, do prayer, maintain discipline, and slowly grow out of dependence.

This shloka also protects us from premature external renunciation. It is better to be an honest karma-yogī than a false renunciate. If the mind is still extroverted, active life can be used for purification. Duties, family responsibilities, work, seva, prayer, and regulated living help the mind mature. Suppression creates pressure; karma-yoga creates purification.
key_verses.reflection:
Where does my outer behavior look spiritual or restrained, while my mind is still dwelling on the same old desires?
Am I trying to suppress a desire without understanding it, regulating it, or growing out of it?
What honest karma-yoga can help me purify the mind instead of pretending to be beyond action?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says that it is not enough to look good on the outside. We also have to be honest inside.
A child may sit at the study table with a book open. From outside, it looks like studying. But inside, the child may be thinking only about games, snacks, or cartoons. Then the body is sitting near the book, but the mind is somewhere else.
Kṛṣṇa is teaching Arjuna: “Do not only stop your hands and legs. Check what your mind is doing.”
If we are not ready to sit quietly, it is better to do a good action. We can help, study, clean, pray, or do our duty properly. Slowly the mind becomes better. Real goodness means the outside and inside should match.
kids_content.story:
Nikhil and the Open Book
Nikhil had a spelling test on Monday. On Sunday evening, his father said, “Nikhil, please study your words for twenty minutes.”
Nikhil wanted to play a video game. But he knew his father would check on him. So he opened his spelling notebook, placed a pencil in his hand, and sat very straight at the table.
From the kitchen, his mother looked at him and smiled. “Good, Nikhil is studying.”
But Nikhil was not studying. His eyes were on the page, but his mind was inside the video game. He imagined the cars racing, the points increasing, the music playing, and the next level opening. Sometimes he even moved his fingers as though he was pressing buttons.
After twenty minutes, his father came and asked, “Ready for a small test?”
Nikhil’s stomach tightened. His father asked the first word. Nikhil stared blankly. He could not spell it. His father asked the second word. Again, Nikhil did not know.
His father sat beside him and said gently, “Your body was at the study table, but where was your mind?”
Nikhil whispered, “In the game.”
Father said, “That is why the study did not help. It is better to study for ten minutes with the mind than sit for twenty minutes only with the body.”
Nikhil closed his eyes for a moment, took a breath, and said, “I will really study now.” This time he read each word aloud, wrote it, and checked it. After some time, he knew the words. His face became bright.
Nikhil learned that pretending is not the same as doing. Kṛṣṇa teaches the same thing. If the body looks controlled but the mind is running behind sense objects, that is not true discipline. The outside and inside should slowly become one.
kids_content.reflection:
When I sit to study, pray, or listen, is my mind really there?
What is one time I only looked like I was doing the right thing, but my mind was somewhere else?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: External restraint
Phrase: karmendriyāṇi saṁyamya = having restrained the organs of action The karmendriyāṇi are the organs of action. A person may physically restrain them. He may stop outward activity, withdraw from duties, or avoid visible sense pursuits. But this alone does not prove inner maturity.
Step 2: Remaining outwardly withdrawn
Phrase: ya āste = who remains / who sits The person may remain outwardly inactive. The posture may look quiet. Society may think he is disciplined or contemplative. But Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa asks us to look deeper than posture.
Step 3: Mental dwelling
Phrase: manasā smaran = remembering / dwelling mentally The real issue is the mind. If the mind continues to dwell on sense objects, then external restraint has not become freedom. The body has withdrawn, but the mind is still moving toward pleasure.
Step 4: Sense objects
Phrase: indriyārthān = sense objects Indriyārthāḥ are objects of the senses — forms, sounds, tastes, touches, smells, and the pleasures associated with them. A person may not physically pursue them, but mentally replay, imagine, crave, and enjoy them.
Step 5: Deluded inner condition
Phrase: vimūḍhātmā = one whose mind is deluded The person is confused about his own condition. He may think, “I am detached,” but the mind reveals otherwise. The delusion is not merely lack of information; it is self-deception.
Step 6: Hypocrisy
Phrase: mithyācāraḥ sa ucyate = he is called a hypocrite Mithyācāraḥ means one whose conduct is false or inconsistent. The outside says renunciation; the inside says attachment. Such a person has a split personality: externally dharma and mokṣa, internally artha and kāma.
Step 7: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka This verse warns against premature withdrawal and suppression. The goal is not to forcibly stop action while mentally feeding desire. The goal is to mature through karma-yoga, regulate the senses, purify the mind, and then become fit for jñāna-yoga. Arjuna should not choose inaction; he should choose honest karma-yoga.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Kālanemi Pretends to Be a Sage (Rāmāyaṇa)
During the Rāmāyaṇa war, Lakṣmaṇa was struck down, and Hanumān was sent to bring the life-restoring herb from the mountain. Rāvaṇa knew that if Hanumān succeeded, Lakṣmaṇa would live and Rāma’s side would regain strength. So he sent the demon Kālanemi to delay Hanumān.
Kālanemi did not stand in Hanumān’s path as a warrior. He created an āśrama-like setting and appeared as a holy sage. The place looked peaceful. The outer appearance suggested tapas, wisdom, and hospitality. When Hanumān came, Kālanemi spoke sweetly and offered rest and refreshment.
But inside, there was no holiness. His mind was not in dharma. His intention was deception. He wanted to delay Hanumān and serve Rāvaṇa’s destructive purpose.
Hanumān eventually recognized the deception. The false sage was exposed and destroyed. The āśrama appearance collapsed into its real meaning: a trap.
This story fits 3.6 because it shows how dangerous outer religious appearance can become when the inner mind is impure. Kālanemi’s form suggested restraint and sanctity, but his mind was dwelling in adharma. Such mismatch between appearance and inner condition is mithyācāra.

2. The Cat Who Became a False Ascetic (Traditional Story)
An old cat could no longer catch birds and mice easily. His body had become slow, but his hunger remained. So he invented a clever plan.
He stood near the riverbank on one leg, closed his eyes, and held his paws in a posture of prayer. He announced to the small animals, “I have become a great ascetic. I no longer harm anyone. I live only for dharma.”
At first, the birds and mice were afraid. But day after day, they saw him standing quietly. They heard him speaking sweet words about non-violence. Slowly they trusted him.
Then some of them came near for blessings. The cat blessed them, praised them, and when no one was watching, ate them.
His outer posture was tapas. His inner mind was appetite. His stillness was not self-control; it was strategy. His religious words were not truth; they were bait.
This story illustrates the shloka vividly. Merely holding the body still does not make the mind pure. A posture can hide desire. A quiet face can hide craving. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa warns that such false conduct is mithyācāra.

3. The Crane Who Pretended to Protect the Fish (Pañcatantra)
A crane lived near a pond full of fish. As he grew old, he could no longer catch fish quickly. So he stood sadly near the pond, pretending to be compassionate.
The fish asked, “Why are you so sorrowful?”
The crane replied, “I have heard that this pond will soon dry up. I am grieving for all of you. If you trust me, I can carry you one by one to a larger lake.”
The frightened fish believed him. One by one, they climbed into his beak. The crane flew away, not to another lake, but to a rock where he ate them.
Finally, a crab asked to be taken. The crane carried him too. But as they flew, the crab saw the bones of the fish below. He understood the deception. With his claws, he caught the crane’s neck and killed him.

The crane’s outer behavior looked compassionate. His inner intention was hunger. He was not protecting the fish; he was using their trust. This story shows the same principle as 3.6: outer conduct without inner purity becomes false conduct. True dharma requires alignment between mind and action.

4. Bhasmāsura’s Tapas Hides a Dangerous Desire (Purāṇic Story)
Bhasmāsura performed severe tapas to please Śiva. From outside, tapas looks noble. It suggests discipline, austerity, and spiritual longing. But tapas can be used for different intentions depending on the mind behind it.
Śiva appeared and offered him a boon. Bhasmāsura asked for a terrible power: whoever’s head he touched should turn to ashes. The request revealed his inner condition. His austerity was not for wisdom, purification, or devotion. It was driven by power and destructive desire.
Once he received the boon, he even tried to test it on Śiva himself. The outer tapas had not purified him. His inner mind was still ruled by arrogance and harm. Finally, Viṣṇu appeared as Mohinī and skillfully led him to place his own hand on his head, destroying him by the very boon he sought.
This story teaches that even religious discipline can become dangerous if the mind is not purified. The shloka’s warning is not limited to sense pleasure alone; it points to the larger danger of outer spirituality with inner impurity. Without inner transformation, external discipline may become mithyācāra.

5. The Blue Jackal Forgets His Real Voice (Pañcatantra)
A jackal once fell into a vat of blue dye while running through a village. When he came out, his body was completely blue. The forest animals had never seen such a creature before. They were frightened and amazed.
The jackal saw an opportunity. He declared, “I am a special being sent by the gods to rule over you.” The animals believed him. Lions, tigers, deer, and birds accepted him as king. He sat proudly, enjoying honor and power.
But one night, he heard other jackals howling in the distance. His old instinct rose. Forgetting his royal pose, he began to howl like a jackal. At once, the animals understood the truth. The outer blue color had hidden him for some time, but his real nature came out through his own voice.
This story is not about sannyāsa, but it powerfully illustrates false appearance. When the outer image does not match the inner nature, the truth eventually appears. In the same way, a person may appear restrained or spiritual, but if the mind is still absorbed in sense objects, the mismatch cannot remain hidden forever. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa calls this mithyācāra.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य यः आस्ते मनसा स्मरन् । इन्द्रियार्थान् विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः सः उच्यते ॥३.६॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: कर्मेन्द्रियाणि = organs of action | संयम्य = having restrained | यः = who | आस्ते = remains, sits | मनसा = by the mind, mentally | स्मरन् = remembering, dwelling upon | इन्द्रियार्थान् = sense objects | विमूढात्मा = deluded person | मिथ्याचारः = hypocrite, one of false conduct | सः = he | उच्यते = is called
rich_grammar.anvayah: यः कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य मनसा इन्द्रियार्थान् स्मरन् आस्ते, सः विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः उच्यते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: कर्म + इन्द्रियाणि = कर्मेन्द्रियाणि | यः + आस्ते = य | इन्द्रिय + अर्थान् = इन्द्रियार्थान् | इन्द्रियार्थान् + विमूढात्मा = इन्द्रियार्थान्विमूढात्मा | विमूढ + आत्मा = विमूढात्मा | मिथ्याचारः + सः = मिथ्याचारः | सः + उच्यते = स
rich_grammar.samasa: कर्मेन्द्रियाणि = कर्मणाम् इन्द्रियाणि Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; the organs of action. | इन्द्रियार्थान् = इन्द्रियाणाम् अर्थान् Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; objects of the senses. | विमूढात्मा = विमूढः आत्मा यस्य सः Samāsa name: बहुव्रीहि समासः; one whose mind/self is deluded. | मिथ्याचारः = मिथ्या आचारः यस्य सः Samāsa name: बहुव्रीहि समासः; one whose conduct is false or hypocritical.
rich_grammar.other: संयम्य is a ल्यबन्त-कृदन्तम् from सम् + यम्, meaning “having restrained.” | स्मरन् is a शतृप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from स्मृ, meaning “remembering” or “dwelling upon.” It qualifies the person indicated by यः. | आस्ते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, from आस्, meaning “sits,” “remains,” or “continues.” | उच्यते is लट्-लकारः, कर्मणि प्रयोगः, from वच्, meaning “is called.” | मनसा is तृतीया विभक्ति, indicating the instrument: “by the mind” or “mentally.” | कर्मेन्द्रियाणि is द्वितीया बहुवचनम् because it is the object of संयम्य. | इन्द्रियार्थान् is द्वितीया बहुवचनम् because it is the object of स्मरन्. | विमूढात्मा and मिथ्याचारः are प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग, describing the same person indicated by सः.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Outward restraint without inner discipline is self-deception. True spiritual growth requires alignment between mind and action. When the heart secretly craves what the body pretends to renounce, the result is confusion, not liberation.

key_verses.title: The True Karma Yogī
key_verses.sanskrit:
यस्त्विन्द्रियाणि मनसा नियम्यारभतेऽर्जुन ।
कर्मेन्द्रियैः(ख्) कर्मयोगम् असक्तः(स्) स विशिष्यते ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
yastvindriyāṇi manasā niyamyārabhate’rjuna ।
karmendriyaiḥ(kh) karmayogam asaktaḥ(s) sa viśiṣyate ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: The one who regulates the sense organs through the mind and performs karma-yoga through the organs of action, without attachment, is superior.
key_verses.concise_journey: This shloka gives the positive alternative to the false renunciate described in the previous shloka. In 3.6, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa warned against a person who outwardly restrains the organs of action but mentally dwells on sense objects. Such a person becomes a mithyācāraḥ, one whose outer life and inner mind do not match. Now, in 3.7, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa praises the sincere karma-yogī.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This shloka gives the positive alternative to the false renunciate described in the previous shloka. In 3.6, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa warned against a person who outwardly restrains the organs of action but mentally dwells on sense objects. Such a person becomes a mithyācāraḥ, one whose outer life and inner mind do not match. Now, in 3.7, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa praises the sincere karma-yogī.

The ideal for most people is not premature withdrawal from life, but active life lived with regulation and detachment. A person may live in society, in family, in work, and in responsibilities. But the sense organs should be regulated by the mind. This means one may fulfill legitimate artha and kāma, but only within dharma. The issue is not whether one lives in the world; the issue is whether one lives in the world with discipline.

Grihastha life is especially suitable for karma-yoga because it gives scope for contribution. It allows one to serve family, society, guests, elders, children, teachers, and dharma. It also gives a lawful space for limited artha-kāma pursuits. But these should not become the main goal of life. They are secondary. Dharma and contribution must remain central.

The phrase asaktaḥ is crucial. The karma-yogī performs action without clinging. He uses relationships, possessions, duties, and opportunities as means for growth, not as sources of permanent security. One must gradually grow out of dependence on everything one uses. If one does not voluntarily grow out of dependence, life will eventually separate one from those very things.

Such a karma-yogī excels. He is superior to an immature renunciate who has given up action externally but is unable to pursue Vedānta and unable to fulfill worldly desires legitimately. Sannyāsa is wonderful for one who loves Vedānta and is inwardly ready. But for one who is not ready, active karma-yoga is healthier, safer, and spiritually superior.
key_verses.story:
Puṇḍalika Serves His Parents While Bhagavān Waits (Vārkarī Tradition)
In the Vārkarī tradition, Puṇḍalika is remembered as a devotee whose service to his parents became worship of Bhagavān. His home was not a forest āśrama. It was not a temple with bells, lamps, and priests. It was an ordinary home where elderly parents needed care.
Puṇḍalika served them with full attention. He washed their feet, arranged their resting place, brought what they needed, and cared for them with reverence. His hands were busy. His body was active. But the action was not merely household work. It was karma-yoga because it was done with devotion and without self-centeredness.
One day Bhagavān Viṭṭhala came to bless him. The Lord stood at Puṇḍalika’s doorway. Imagine the moment: Bhagavān himself had arrived, yet Puṇḍalika was in the middle of serving his parents. He did not disrespect Bhagavān, and he did not abandon the duty he was performing. He placed a brick for Bhagavān to stand on and asked him to wait until the service was complete.
Bhagavān waited.
This is the beauty of the story. Puṇḍalika’s action was so pure that Bhagavān did not see it as a delay. He saw it as worship. The Lord standing on the brick became the beloved form of Viṭṭhala of Paṇḍharpur.
This story illumines 3.7 perfectly. Puṇḍalika did not run away from action to become spiritual. He used the organs of action in service. He regulated his life around duty and devotion. He did not treat family responsibility as an obstacle to Bhagavān; he transformed it into an offering to Bhagavān.
The sincere karma-yogī excels because his action purifies the mind. Such action is far superior to empty withdrawal or false spirituality.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka is a powerful correction for modern spiritual confusion. We may think spirituality means withdrawing from work, family, money, people, and responsibility. But for most of us, the field of growth is exactly where we are — home, school, workplace, community, temple, and society.

A parent cooking daily for the family can practice karma-yoga. A student studying sincerely can practice karma-yoga. A professional earning honestly and helping others can practice karma-yoga. A person caring for elders can practice karma-yoga. A volunteer serving in a temple, gurukula, hospital, or food-seva can practice karma-yoga.

But the action must be regulated. Grihastha life allows artha and kāma, but not without limits. Entertainment, money, comfort, status, and relationships must remain within dharma. Regulation is not suppression; it is dharmic guidance of the senses. It means asking: Is this right? Is this moderate? Is this helpful? Is this aligned with dharma?

The verse also teaches detachment. Detachment does not mean neglect. We can love family, do duty, earn, serve, study, and enjoy legitimate pleasures. But we should not make anything finite into our ultimate support. Everything we use should become a means for maturity, not a chain of dependence.

For current life, the practical message is: do not pretend to be beyond action; do not run away from duty; regulate the senses; serve; contribute; act without clinging; and let action purify the mind for knowledge.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I using my present duties as karma-yoga, or am I seeing them only as burdens?
Where do I need more regulation in artha, kāma, entertainment, food, speech, or comfort?
Can I perform one important duty today without complaint and without expecting appreciation?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says that doing nothing is not always better. It is better to do the right thing in the right way.
A child has hands, legs, eyes, ears, and lots of energy. The question is: how will the child use them? Hands can hit, or hands can help. The mouth can complain, or the mouth can speak kindly. The eyes can keep looking at distractions, or the eyes can look at the book while studying.
Kṛṣṇa tells Arjuna, “Use your body and mind properly. Do your duty, but do not get stuck to what you want all the time.”
So when we study, help at home, clean our things, pray, or speak kindly, we are using our body in a good way. That is better than just sitting quietly while the mind wants many things.
kids_content.story:
Tara Uses Her Hands Well
Tara loved drawing. Every afternoon after school, she would take out her colors and fill pages with flowers, houses, birds, and stars. One day, her mother said, “Tara, before you draw, please help me fold these clean clothes.”
Tara looked at the pile. It was big. Socks, towels, shirts, and little handkerchiefs were all mixed together.
She made a face. “But Amma, I want to draw. Folding clothes is boring.”
Mother said, “You may draw after this. First let us finish this small job.”
Tara sat down, but her hands moved slowly. She folded one towel and sighed loudly. Then she folded another and said, “Why do I have to do this?”
Her grandmother, who was sitting nearby, smiled and said, “Tara, your hands are wonderful. They can draw beautiful pictures. They can also help the family. When hands help, the heart becomes bigger.”
Tara looked at her hands. She imagined them drawing a flower. Then she imagined them helping Amma. She picked up a shirt and folded it neatly. Then another. Then she matched the socks. Soon the pile became smaller.
Mother said, “Thank you, Tara. You helped me a lot.”
Tara felt happy. Then she went to draw. This time she drew a picture of two hands holding a bright flower.
Tara learned that hands are not only for doing what we like. Hands can also do what is helpful. Kṛṣṇa teaches Arjuna the same thing: use the body for good action, with a steady mind and a kind heart.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one helpful thing I can do with my hands today?
When I do a job I do not like, can I do it without making a face or complaining?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Contrast with the previous shloka
Phrase: yaḥ tu = but the one who The word tu marks a contrast. In 3.6, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa criticized the false renunciate. Now he praises the sincere karma-yogī. The contrast is between hypocrisy and honest action.
Step 2: Regulating the senses
Phrase: indriyāṇi manasā niyamya = having regulated the sense organs by the mind The sense organs must be regulated, not violently suppressed. The mind must guide them with discrimination. In active life, one may have legitimate contact with the world, but the senses should not violate dharma or indulge without limit.
Step 3: Beginning karma-yoga
Phrase: ārabhate’rjuna = undertakes, O Arjuna Bhagavān directly addresses Arjuna. The instruction is not to escape activity, but to undertake karma-yoga. Arjuna must engage in the right action with the right attitude.
Step 4: Using the organs of action
Phrase: karmendriyaiḥ = with the organs of action The organs of action are to be used, not merely shut down. Speech, hands, feet, and bodily capacities can become instruments of service, duty, worship, and contribution. Action becomes purifying when performed as karma-yoga.
Step 5: Karma-yoga as the discipline
Phrase: karmayogam = karma-yoga Karma-yoga here is not mere busyness. It is dhārmic action done for purification. It includes contribution, service, duty, and discipline. In the grihastha context, this means living in society in a way that contributes more than it consumes.
Step 6: Without attachment
Phrase: asaktaḥ = without attachment The karma-yogī acts without clinging. He may use possessions, relationships, roles, and opportunities, but he gradually grows out of dependence on them. Detachment is not neglect; it is freedom from slavery to what one uses.
Step 7: Such a person excels
Phrase: sa viśiṣyate = he excels Such a karma-yogī is superior to the immature renunciate described in the previous shloka. A sincere grihastha practicing karma-yoga is better than a person who outwardly withdraws but inwardly longs for worldly objects.
Step 8: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse teaches that active life, when disciplined and unattached, is not an obstacle. It is the proper field for most people to purify the mind. Arjuna should not choose premature inaction. He should live a life of regulated senses, active duty, contribution, and detachment.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Lakṣmaṇa Builds the Forest Hut with Devotion (Rāmāyaṇa)
When Rāma, Sītā, and Lakṣmaṇa entered forest life, they did not have the comforts of Ayodhyā. There were no palace rooms, no attendants, no royal kitchens, and no guards. The forest was beautiful, but it was also uncertain. They needed a place to live.
At Pañcavaṭī, Rāma asked Lakṣmaṇa to prepare a dwelling. Lakṣmaṇa did not treat the work as low or ordinary. He gathered wood, leaves, grass, and branches. His hands worked carefully. His feet moved through the forest. His mind was alert to what would be suitable for Rāma and Sītā.
He built the parṇaśālā with love and attention. This was not just construction. It was service. His action was guided by devotion and dharma. He did not say, “I came to the forest, so I should sit quietly.” He used his body, strength, and skill for the right duty.
This story reflects 3.7. Lakṣmaṇa regulated his senses and used the organs of action in service. He was active, but not selfishly active. His action was karma-yoga because it was done for dharma, with dedication and without personal demand.

2. Vidura Leaves the Court Without Clinging to Position (Mahābhārata)
Vidura lived in the Kuru court, surrounded by politics, ambition, jealousy, and injustice. He repeatedly warned Dhṛtarāṣṭra that Duryodhana’s path would destroy the family. He spoke truth even when truth was unwelcome.
At one point, Duryodhana insulted Vidura harshly. Vidura had served the kingdom with loyalty and wisdom, but when adharma rejected his counsel, he did not cling to his position. He did not beg for respect. He did not compromise truth to remain in the palace. He quietly laid down his bow and left.
This was not weakness. It was detachment. Vidura had acted fully while action was required. He advised, warned, served, and spoke dharma. But when the court chose adharma and insulted him, he showed that his identity did not depend on title, palace, or recognition.
This story fits 3.7 because Vidura was active in society, yet inwardly unattached. He did not run away from duty prematurely, and he did not cling when the time came to leave. His speech and action were governed by dharma, not by ego.

3. Kannappa’s Active Devotion Becomes Worship (Śaiva Nāyaṉmār Tradition)
Kannappa was a hunter, not a priest trained in temple ritual. His life was active and rough. He moved through the forest, carried a bow, and lived by hunting. But when devotion to Śiva awakened in him, that same active nature was transformed.
He found a Śiva-liṅga in the forest and began worshipping in the only way he knew. He brought water in his mouth because he had no vessel. He offered food from what he had. He guarded the liṅga with fierce love. Externally, his worship did not look refined. But his heart was direct and total.
Then he saw something that shook him: one eye of the liṅga appeared to be bleeding. Kannappa could not bear the sight. His devotion was not theoretical. He was ready to offer everything. As he prepared to give even his own eye in devotion, Bhagavān Śiva revealed himself and blessed him.
The story shows that the organs of action can become sacred when guided by devotion. Kannappa did not suppress his active nature. He transformed it. His hands, feet, mouth, and body became instruments of offering. This is the spirit of karma-yoga: action purified by devotion and freedom from selfish calculation.

4. Sudāmā Offers Without Demand (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Sudāmā lived in poverty with his wife and children. He had studied with Kṛṣṇa in the gurukula, but he did not use that relationship for gain. His life was simple, and his means were small.
One day, his wife gently urged him to visit Kṛṣṇa. Not for luxury, but because the family was suffering. Sudāmā hesitated. He did not want to ask. Finally, he agreed to go. His wife found a small quantity of flattened rice and tied it in a cloth as a humble gift.
When Sudāmā reached Dvārakā, Kṛṣṇa did not receive him as a poor man. He ran to him with love, embraced him, seated him with honor, and washed his feet. Sudāmā was overwhelmed. He had brought almost nothing, yet Kṛṣṇa treated it as precious.
Sudāmā did not ask for wealth. He offered what he had and accepted Kṛṣṇa’s love. When he returned home, his hut had become a beautiful dwelling by Kṛṣṇa’s grace. Still, his heart remained simple and unattached.
This story illustrates asaktaḥ. Sudāmā acted: he traveled, offered, met Kṛṣṇa, and returned. But he did not cling, demand, bargain, or calculate. His action was humble and free from grasping. This is the inner fragrance of karma-yoga.

5. Nandanār Receives Darśana at Tiruppuṅkūr (Śaiva Nāyaṉmār Tradition)
Nandanār lived a humble life filled with work and social hardship, but his heart longed for Śiva. He deeply desired darśana of Bhagavān at sacred temples. His outer life was constrained, but his devotion was free.
At Tiruppuṅkūr, he stood outside longing to see the Śiva-liṅga. But Nandi stood in the line of sight, blocking his view. Nandanār did not become angry. He did not demand recognition. He stood with longing and humility.
His prayer was simple and intense: he wanted only to see Bhagavān. By Śiva’s grace, Nandi moved aside, allowing Nandanār to have darśana.
This story shows how even a life of humble action can become spiritually radiant when the mind is directed toward Bhagavān. Nandanār did not possess status or outward freedom. Yet his work, longing, humility, and devotion purified the heart.
This connects to 3.7 because karma-yoga is not limited to people in comfortable situations. The sincere seeker uses the life available to him. With regulated senses, devotion, humility, and non-demanding action, even ordinary work becomes a path of purification.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: यः तु इन्द्रियाणि मनसा नियम्य आरभते अर्जुन । कर्मेन्द्रियैः कर्मयोगम् असक्तः सः विशिष्यते ॥३.७॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: यः = who | तु = but | इन्द्रियाणि = sense organs | मनसा = by the mind | नियम्य = having regulated | आरभते = undertakes, begins | अर्जुन = O Arjuna | कर्मेन्द्रियैः = by the organs of action | कर्मयोगम् = karma-yoga | असक्तः = unattached | सः = he | विशिष्यते = excels, is superior
rich_grammar.anvayah: हे अर्जुन, यः तु मनसा इन्द्रियाणि नियम्य असक्तः कर्मेन्द्रियैः कर्मयोगम् आरभते, सः विशिष्यते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: यः + तु = यस्तु | यस्तु + इन्द्रियाणि = यस्त्विन्द्रियाणि | नियम्य + आरभते = नियम्यारभते | आरभते + अर्जुन = आरभतेऽर्जुन | कर्म + इन्द्रियैः = कर्मेन्द्रियैः | कर्मयोगम् + असक्तः = कर्मयोगमसक्तः | सः + विशिष्यते = स
rich_grammar.samasa: कर्मेन्द्रियैः = कर्मणाम् इन्द्रियैः Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; by the organs of action. | कर्मयोगम् = कर्मणः योगः Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; the discipline connected with action. | असक्तः = न सक्तः This is a negative adjectival expression meaning “not attached.” It is best treated here simply as an adjective rather than forcing a detailed samāsa analysis.
rich_grammar.other: नियम्य is a ल्यबन्त-कृदन्तम् from नि + यम्, meaning “having regulated” or “having controlled.” | आरभते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, from आ + रभ्, meaning “undertakes” or “begins.” | मनसा is तृतीया विभक्ति, indicating the instrument: “by the mind.” | इन्द्रियाणि is द्वितीया बहुवचनम् because it is the object of नियम्य. | कर्मेन्द्रियैः is तृतीया बहुवचनम्, indicating the instruments through which karma-yoga is undertaken. | कर्मयोगम् is द्वितीया एकवचनम्, the object of आरभते. | असक्तः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग, qualifying the person indicated by यः and सः. | विशिष्यते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, from वि + शिष्. It means “is distinguished,” “excels,” or “is superior.” Here it means the sincere karma-yogī is superior to the mithyācāraḥ of the previous shloka.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The true karma-yogī acts with full engagement but without attachment. By regulating the senses through the mind and performing duty as an offering, one transforms everyday action into a path of purification and inner growth.

kids_story.caption: Tara sitting beside a pile of clean clothes, first making a reluctant face, while her grandmother gently points to her hands and explains that hands can help as well as draw

kids_explain.caption: Tara happily folding clothes with her mother and then drawing two hands holding a bright flower, showing helpful action becoming joyful

detailed_meaning.caption: A symbolic scene of sense organs as five lively horses guided by the mind as reins, with the organs of action serving people through karma-yoga, Kṛṣṇa’s light showing detachment
