key_verses.title: Perform Your Prescribed Duty
key_verses.sanskrit:
नियतं(ङ्) कुरु कर्म त्वं(ङ्) कर्म ज्यायो ह्यकर्मणः ।
शरीरयात्रापि च ते न प्रसिद्ध्येदकर्मणः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
niyataṁ(ṅ) kuru karma tvaṁ(ṅ) karma jyāyo hyakarmaṇaḥ ।
śarīrayātrāpi ca te na prasiddhyedakarmaṇaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Perform your prescribed duty, because action is superior to inaction; even the maintenance of your body is not possible through inaction.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now begins the detailed teaching of karma-yoga. Until this point, he has shown why Arjuna should not avoid action: avoiding action does not give liberation, total inaction is impossible, forced inaction can create hypocrisy, and sincere karma-yoga is superior. Now he says directly: niyataṁ karma kuru — perform the action that is required of you.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now begins the detailed teaching of karma-yoga. Until this point, he has shown why Arjuna should not avoid action: avoiding action does not give liberation, total inaction is impossible, forced inaction can create hypocrisy, and sincere karma-yoga is superior. Now he says directly: niyataṁ karma kuru — perform the action that is required of you.

Niyatam karma means prescribed, obligatory, or appropriate action. It includes one’s duties according to one’s role, stage of life, situation, and dharma. For Arjuna, the immediate niyata karma is to stand as a kṣatriya and fight a dhārmic war. For others, it may be studying, earning honestly, caring for family, serving society, worship, charity, teaching, protecting, or helping.

The verse says karma jyāyaḥ akarmaṇaḥ — action is better than inaction. This does not mean any action is better than any inaction. It means dhārmic, prescribed action is better than laziness, escapism, negligence, or avoidance. Inaction may look peaceful, but for an unprepared mind it often becomes tamas, stagnation, dependency, or hidden desire.

Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa gives a simple practical reason: even the journey of the body cannot continue without action. To maintain the body, one must eat, move, clean, work, seek food, protect health, and live responsibly. Even a person who claims to renounce action depends on the action of others for food, shelter, clothing, and care. Therefore, action cannot be dismissed.

This verse also begins the clearer definition of karma-yoga. Karma-yoga is not mere busyness. It is proper action with proper attitude. Proper action means sāttvika action should dominate: giving more than one takes, contributing to others, serving, protecting, and living in alignment with dharma. Rājasa action, done for personal and family needs, may be necessary but should remain limited. Tāmasa action, which harms others or violates dharma, must be avoided. Proper attitude means offering action to Bhagavān and accepting the result as prasāda.

So the teaching is: do not avoid action; do the right action; do it as an offering; receive the result gracefully; and let action purify the mind.
key_verses.story:
King Śibi Protects the Dove (Mahābhārata)
King Śibi was known for protecting those who came to him for refuge. One day, a frightened dove flew into his lap, trembling. Behind it came a hawk, sharp-eyed and hungry. The dove had escaped death and now clung to the king as its only shelter.
The hawk spoke with force: “O king, that dove is my food. If you protect it, you are denying me my rightful meal. Is that dharma?”
Śibi faced a difficult situation. As a king, his duty was to protect one who sought refuge. But he could not ignore the hawk’s hunger either. He did not close his eyes and say, “This is too complicated; I will do nothing.” He did not abandon the dove. He did not dismiss the hawk. He acted.
The king offered his own flesh equal in weight to the dove. A scale was brought. The dove was placed on one side. Śibi cut flesh from his own body and placed it on the other side. But the dove still weighed more. He added more. Still the scale did not balance. Finally, Śibi himself climbed onto the scale, ready to give his whole body to protect the one who had come for refuge.
At that moment, the test was revealed. The dove and hawk were divine beings who had come to test the king’s commitment to dharma. Śibi had shown that niyata karma is not always convenient, easy, or pleasant. But when dharma calls, noble action is better than inaction.
This story illumines 3.8. Śibi did not choose akarma, inaction, when action was required. He performed the duty of a king: protection, justice, and sacrifice. His action was not selfish; it was sāttvika karma. He gave more than he took. Such action, done in dharma, purifies and elevates the mind.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka removes a common misunderstanding: karma-yoga is not avoiding responsibility in the name of spirituality. It is doing the niyata karma that belongs to us, with proper action and proper attitude.

Many people say, “I want peace, so I do not want duties.” But life itself runs on duty. A student must study. A parent must care. A professional must work honestly. A citizen must contribute. A devotee must worship. A human being must maintain the body and serve the world that supports it.

Even basic body maintenance requires action. We must cook or obtain food, clean the home, care for health, earn or arrange resources, and depend on the effort of countless people. If we eat rice, someone grew it, transported it, sold it, cooked it, and served it. If we wear clothes, someone produced them. If we use electricity, roads, temples, schools, hospitals, and books, we are receiving the fruits of others’ actions. To only consume and not contribute is not karma-yoga.

In Indian life, this applies strongly. Taking care of parents, raising children with values, doing one’s work honestly, supporting temples and gurukulas, feeding guests, giving dānam, participating in seva, studying śāstra, and doing daily prayer are not distractions from spirituality. They are part of niyata karma when done with the right attitude.

The practical message is not “do more and more.” It is “do what is yours to do.” Avoid harmful action. Limit selfish action. Increase selfless action. Offer action to Bhagavān. Accept results as prasāda. That is how daily life becomes karma-yoga.
key_verses.reflection:
What is one niyata karma — required duty — that I am avoiding right now?
In my daily actions, am I giving more than I take from family, society, and dharma?
Can I do one ordinary duty today as īśvarārpaṇam and accept the result as prasāda?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa tells Arjuna, “Do the duty that is yours to do.”
Doing nothing is not better. If we do not brush our teeth, the teeth do not become clean. If we do not eat, the body becomes weak. If we do not study, we do not learn. If we do not help at home, someone else has to do all the work.
Every person has some jobs to do. A child studies, keeps things clean, speaks kindly, helps parents, and prays. These are good actions.
Kṛṣṇa is teaching that we should not run away from the right action. We should do it properly, with a good heart.
kids_content.story:
Riya and the Lunchbox
Riya came home from school, dropped her bag near the sofa, and ran to play. Her mother called from the kitchen, “Riya, please take out your lunchbox and put it near the sink.”
Riya shouted, “Later!”
She played with her blocks. Then she colored a picture. Then she watched her little brother build a tower. The lunchbox stayed inside the bag.
In the evening, Amma opened the schoolbag. The smell came out first. The lunchbox had leaked. A little curd rice had spread inside. One notebook corner was wet. Amma did not scold loudly. She called Riya and showed her.
Riya’s face became small. “I forgot.”
Amma said gently, “Taking out your lunchbox is your small duty. If you do it on time, the bag stays clean, the notebook stays safe, and I get help. If you leave it, the work does not disappear. It becomes more work.”
Riya brought a cloth. She helped wipe the bag. She kept the notebook open to dry. Then she washed the lunchbox carefully.
The next day, when she came home, she remembered. Before playing, she opened the bag, took out the lunchbox, and put it near the sink. Amma smiled. Riya felt proud inside.
Riya learned that small duties matter. Kṛṣṇa teaches Arjuna the same thing: do the right action at the right time. Running away from duty only makes life messy.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one small job that is mine to do every day?
What happens when I say “later” and do not do my duty?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Prescribed duty
Phrase: niyataṁ karma = prescribed or required action Niyatam karma means the action that is enjoined, appropriate, or required. It is not random activity. It is the duty that comes from one’s role, situation, stage of life, and dharma.
Step 2: Direct command
Phrase: kuru = do Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa gives a direct instruction. After explaining why action should not be avoided, he now says: do the required action. For Arjuna, this means taking up his kṣatriya duty.
Step 3: Personal responsibility
Phrase: tvaṁ = you The word tvaṁ makes it direct. Arjuna cannot make a general philosophical discussion out of the issue. The question is: what is your duty now? Each person must recognize the duty that belongs to him or her.
Step 4: Action is better than inaction
Phrase: karma jyāyaḥ hi akarmaṇaḥ = action is indeed superior to inaction Here karma means proper prescribed action, not any impulsive activity. Akarma means inaction, avoidance, or non-performance of duty. Dhārmic action is superior to avoidance because it purifies and sustains life.
Step 5: Body maintenance
Phrase: śarīrayātrā api ca te = even the journey/maintenance of your body Śarīra-yātrā means the maintenance or continuation of bodily life. Even the body’s basic functioning depends on action: food, movement, cleaning, protection, and health.
Step 6: Inaction cannot sustain life
Phrase: na prasiddhyet akarmaṇaḥ = it will not be accomplished through inaction Without action, even bodily life cannot be maintained. Therefore, to avoid action in the name of spirituality is unrealistic. One depends on action either directly or through others.
Step 7: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse begins the practical definition of karma-yoga. Perform the action that is required. Let sāttvika action predominate. Let selfish action be limited. Avoid harmful action. Offer action to Bhagavān and receive the result as prasāda. Thus action becomes a means of purification.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Hanumān Leaps to Laṅkā for Rāma’s Work (Rāmāyaṇa)
When the vānaras reached the southern ocean, they faced a terrifying problem. Sītā had to be found, but Laṅkā lay across the vast sea. The vānaras stood on the shore, measuring their strength. Some could leap a little. Some could leap far but not return. The mission seemed impossible.
Hanumān sat quietly, not yet aware of his own strength. Then Jāmbavān reminded him of who he was and what he was capable of. Hanumān rose, expanded in power, and prepared to leap across the ocean.
He did not say, “Let me remain peaceful and do nothing.” He did not avoid the mission because it was difficult. This was Rāma-kārya, the work that had come to him. His body, speech, mind, strength, and courage became instruments of service.
As he leapt across the ocean, obstacles appeared: Maināka mountain offered rest, Surasā tested him, Siṁhikā tried to capture him, and Laṅkā’s guardian stopped him. Hanumān met each situation intelligently and continued. His action was not restless busyness. It was focused, dhārmic, and offered to Rāma.
This story reflects 3.8 because Hanumān performs the required action. When duty appears, inaction is not noble. Proper action, done for dharma and Bhagavān, becomes karma-yoga.

2. Sāvitrī Follows Yama with Determined Dharma (Mahābhārata)
Sāvitrī knew that her husband Satyavān was destined to die within a year. Yet she did not collapse into helplessness. She continued to live with discipline, devotion, and care. On the destined day, she accompanied Satyavān into the forest.
Satyavān became weak while cutting wood and laid his head in Sāvitrī’s lap. There, his life departed, and Yama came to take him. Sāvitrī could have remained frozen in grief. Instead, she rose and followed Yama.
Yama told her to turn back. But Sāvitrī followed with humility and firmness. She spoke words of dharma, wisdom, and devotion. Step by step, Yama granted her boons. She asked not selfishly, but with intelligence and alignment to dharma. Finally, through her steadfastness, Satyavān’s life was restored.
This story shows that dharma often requires action even in grief. Sāvitrī did not choose passive sorrow. She performed the action that belonged to her as a devoted wife and a wise seeker of dharma. Her action was disciplined, respectful, and powerful.
The shloka teaches the same spirit: prescribed duty must be performed. Inaction may look easier, but dhārmic action sustains life, protects values, and purifies the mind.

3. King Dilīpa Serves Nandinī Every Day (Raghuvaṁśa / Purāṇic Tradition)
King Dilīpa longed for a child and approached Sage Vasiṣṭha. The sage instructed him to serve the divine cow Nandinī. This was not a dramatic battlefield duty. It was daily, steady, humble service.
Dilīpa followed Nandinī into the forest every day. When she grazed, he stood guard. When she walked, he walked behind her. When she rested, he watched carefully. A king who was used to royal honor now performed quiet service without complaint.
One day, a lion appeared and seized Nandinī. Dilīpa was horrified. Protecting her had been his duty. He offered himself in her place, ready to give his own body rather than fail in the responsibility given by the guru. Then the test was revealed. Nandinī blessed him.
This story shows the power of niyata karma. The duty may seem small, repetitive, or beneath one’s status, but when it is prescribed by dharma and performed with humility, it purifies. Dilīpa’s daily service became a sacred discipline.
The teaching of 3.8 is not only about heroic action. It includes ordinary, repeated, necessary duty. Do what is yours to do. Do it steadily. Let action become worship.

4. The Squirrel Helps Build Rāma’s Bridge (Rāmāyaṇa Tradition)
When Rāma’s bridge to Laṅkā was being built, the vānaras carried huge rocks and trees. The work was massive. The ocean had to be crossed, and everyone was engaged in Rāma’s mission.
Amid the great vānaras, a tiny squirrel also wanted to help. It rolled in the sand, ran to the bridge, shook off the grains, and returned for more. Compared with the mountains carried by Hanumān and others, the squirrel’s contribution looked tiny.
Some laughed at it. But Rāma noticed. He gently touched the squirrel and blessed it. The small lines seen on the squirrel’s back are traditionally remembered as Rāma’s loving touch.
The squirrel did not say, “My work is too small, so I will do nothing.” It did what it could. Its action was appropriate to its capacity and offered with devotion. That is karma-yoga.
This story beautifully reflects 3.8. Niyata karma is not always grand. Sometimes it is the small duty that belongs to us. Action done with devotion is better than inaction justified by comparison.

5. Draupadī Calls for Help and Does Not Give Up (Mahābhārata)
In the Kuru assembly, Draupadī was dragged into a terrible situation after the dice game. Elders were present, kings were present, and yet dharma seemed silent. She did not sink into passive despair. She questioned the court. She asked whether Yudhiṣṭhira had the right to stake her after losing himself. She appealed to dharma.
When human help failed, she turned fully to Bhagavān. Her hands rose in surrender, and she called upon Kṛṣṇa. Her prayer was not laziness. It came after she had questioned, resisted, and stood for dharma with all the strength available to her.
Her action was both human effort and surrender. She did not remain inert. She did what dharma demanded: she spoke, questioned, appealed, and finally surrendered to Bhagavān.
This story connects to 3.8 because action is required even in crisis. Sometimes the action is physical; sometimes it is speech; sometimes it is prayerful surrender. Akarma is not the answer. Dhārmic action, performed with trust in Bhagavān, is the path.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: नियतम् कुरु कर्म त्वम् कर्म ज्यायः हि अकर्मणः । शरीरयात्रा अपि च ते न प्रसिद्ध्येत् अकर्मणः ॥३.८॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: नियतम् = prescribed, obligatory, regulated | कुरु = do | कर्म = action, duty | त्वम् = you | कर्म = action | ज्यायः = superior, better | हि = indeed | अकर्मणः = than inaction | शरीरयात्रा = maintenance of the body | अपि = even | च = and | ते = for you / your | न = not | प्रसिद्ध्येत् = would be accomplished, would succeed | अकर्मणः = through inaction
rich_grammar.anvayah: त्वं नियतं कर्म कुरु । हि कर्म अकर्मणः ज्यायः । अकर्मणः ते शरीरयात्रा अपि च न प्रसिद्ध्येत् ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: नियतम् + कुरु = नियतं | ज्यायः + हि = ज्यायो | हि + अकर्मणः = ह्यकर्मणः | शरीरयात्रा + अपि = शरीरयात्रापि | प्रसिद्ध्येत् + अकर्मणः = प्रसिद्ध्येदकर्मणः
rich_grammar.samasa: शरीरयात्रा = शरीरस्य यात्रा Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; the maintenance or continuation of the body. | अकर्म = न कर्म Samāsa name: नञ्-तत्पुरुष समासः; non-action or inaction. | नियतम् is used here as an adjective qualifying कर्म. It means prescribed, regulated, or obligatory.
rich_grammar.other: कुरु is लोट्-लकारः from कृ, a direct imperative: “do.” | नियतम् is द्वितीया एकवचनम् नपुंसकलिङ्ग, agreeing with कर्म. | The first कर्म is द्वितीया एकवचनम्, the object of कुरु. | The second कर्म is प्रथमा एकवचनम्, the subject of the comparison: “action is superior.” | अकर्मणः is पञ्चमी विभक्ति in both occurrences. In the first, it is used in comparison with ज्यायः: “better than inaction.” In the second, it indicates “from/by inaction” in the sense that bodily maintenance is not accomplished through inaction. | ज्यायः is a comparative form meaning “better” or “superior.” | प्रसिद्ध्येत् is विधिलिङ्, आत्मनेपदम्, from प्र + सिध्, meaning “would be accomplished” or “would succeed.” | ते is best understood here as “for you” or “your,” connected with शरीरयात्रा: “even your bodily maintenance.”

key_verses.final_takeaway: Action performed as duty is superior to inaction. Even maintaining the body requires effort. Rather than abandoning action in the hope of peace, one should perform prescribed duties with discipline and the right attitude.

key_verses.title: Action as Sacred Offering
key_verses.sanskrit:
यज्ञार्थात्कर्मणोऽन्यत्र लोकोऽयं(ङ्) कर्मबन्धनः ।
तदर्थं(ङ्) कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः(स्) समाचर ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
yajñārthātkarmaṇo’nyatra loko’yaṁ(ṅ) karmabandhanaḥ ।
tadarthaṁ(ṅ) karma kaunteya muktasaṅgaḥ(s) samācara ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Actions done for yajña liberate; actions done without that offering attitude bind, so perform action for that yajña, free from attachment.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now gives the heart of karma-yoga. In the previous verse, he said, “Do your niyata karma.” Now he explains how that action must be done so that it becomes spiritually purifying rather than binding.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now gives the heart of karma-yoga. In the previous verse, he said, “Do your niyata karma.” Now he explains how that action must be done so that it becomes spiritually purifying rather than binding.

The key word is yajña. Here yajña does not mean only a fire ritual. It means a life of offering, worship, contribution, reverence, and harmony. When work is done for Bhagavān, for dharma, for the welfare of the whole, and with an attitude of contribution, that action becomes yajña. When work is done only for ego, possession, enjoyment, control, or personal gain, the same action becomes bondage.

Action itself is neutral. It can bind or liberate depending on how it is handled. Food cooked with selfishness can strengthen ego; food cooked as service can become worship. Earning money with greed can bind; earning honestly and sharing can purify. Speaking can hurt; speaking kindly can heal. The difference is not merely the outer action, but the attitude behind it.

A yajña-life also means recognizing that we are not isolated individuals. We receive endlessly from the world: air, water, earth, sunlight, food, parents, teachers, society, scriptures, culture, and countless people whose names we do not know. Since we receive, we must also contribute. If we only take and do not give back, we disturb the harmony of life. If we live as contributors, our life becomes an offering.

Therefore, Bhagavān says: do action for yajña. Be muktasaṅgaḥ — free from attachment. Do not act with the tight feeling “This is only for me, my comfort, my name, my success.” Act as an offering. Then karma becomes karma-yoga.
key_verses.story:
The Squirrel Helps Build Rāma’s Bridge (Rāmāyaṇa Tradition)
When Śrī Rāma’s army reached the ocean, Laṅkā stood far away across the waters. Sītā had to be rescued, but the sea was vast. The vānaras began the great work of building the bridge. Huge rocks and trees were carried by powerful warriors. The beach was filled with movement, shouting, effort, and devotion.
In the middle of this enormous work, a tiny squirrel watched. It could not lift mountains like Hanumān. It could not carry trees like the great vānaras. But it too wanted to serve Rāma.
So the little squirrel did what it could. It rolled in the sand, ran to the bridge, shook the grains of sand from its body, and ran back for more. Again and again it carried tiny grains. Compared to the boulders, its contribution looked almost invisible.
Some vānaras laughed. “What are these few grains of sand compared to our rocks?”
But Rāma noticed. He saw not the size of the contribution, but the spirit behind it. The squirrel was not working for fame. It was not comparing. It was not demanding recognition. It was offering itself to Rāma’s work. Rāma gently stroked the squirrel, blessing it with affection.
This story beautifully illustrates yajña. Yajña is not measured by size. It is measured by the attitude of offering. A great action done with ego can bind; a small action done as worship can purify. The squirrel’s tiny service became sacred because it was done for Rāma, without attachment and without self-importance.
This is the teaching of 3.9: act for yajña, act as an offering, and the same action that could have been ordinary becomes liberating.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka changes how we look at daily life. Karma-yoga is not just doing duties. It is doing duties as yajña — as an offering to Bhagavān and as a contribution to the harmony of life.

We receive from everywhere. The food on our plate comes from soil, rain, sunlight, farmers, transporters, shopkeepers, and the person who cooked it. The house we live in, the roads we use, the language we speak, the books we study, the temple we visit, the family that supports us — all are gifts. A mature life asks: “What am I giving back?”

In Indian life, yajña can take many forms. Feeding guests, caring for parents, teaching children, supporting gurukulas, doing annadānam, protecting nature, avoiding pollution, paying workers fairly, doing one’s profession honestly, praying before eating, and sharing knowledge can all become yajña when done with the right attitude.

The shloka also warns us. The same action can bind if the attitude is selfish. Cooking can bind if it is filled with complaint and expectation. Work can bind if it is only for ego and comparison. Charity can bind if it is done for name and recognition. Even religious activity can bind if it is done only for status.

The practical message is: convert work into worship. Before action, offer it to Bhagavān. During action, do it carefully. After action, accept the result as prasāda. Then action does not become a chain; it becomes a purifier.
key_verses.reflection:
Is my daily work mainly for taking, or is it also a contribution?
Which ordinary action can I consciously convert into yajña today?
Can I do one action for Bhagavān without expecting appreciation, recognition, or return?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says, “Do your actions as an offering.”
When we help only because we want praise, we may feel upset if nobody says thank you. But when we help as an offering to Bhagavān, the heart feels happy even if nobody notices.
Even small actions can become special. Giving water to a plant, helping Amma set the table, sharing a pencil, cleaning up toys, saying a kind word, or praying before eating can all become offerings.
Kṛṣṇa teaches Arjuna: “Do your duty, but do not make it only about you. Do it for Bhagavān. Do it to help.”
kids_content.story:
Aarav’s One Cup of Water
Aarav had a small tulasī plant near the window. Every morning, his grandmother would water it, light a lamp, and say a short prayer. Aarav usually watched for a moment and then ran away to play.
One morning, grandmother had a headache. She sat quietly on the sofa and said, “Aarav, can you water the tulasī today?”
Aarav was building a toy car track. He almost said, “Later.” But then he looked at the plant. The leaves were slightly drooping.
He took a small cup, filled it carefully, and walked slowly so it would not spill. He poured the water near the roots. Then he folded his hands and said, “Bhagavān, this is for you.”
Nobody clapped. Nobody gave him a prize. But Aarav felt happy inside.
The next day, he watered the plant again. Soon it became his little morning offering. He learned that even one cup of water can become special when it is given with love.
Kṛṣṇa teaches the same thing. A small action done as an offering becomes yajña.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one small thing I can do today as an offering to Bhagavān?
Can I help someone without waiting for praise?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Action for yajña
Phrase: yajñārthāt karmaṇaḥ = from action done for the sake of yajña Yajña here means offering to Bhagavān, worship, contribution, and living in harmony with the larger order. Action done for yajña is action done with devotion and contribution.
Step 2: Other than yajña
Phrase: anyatra = otherwise, other than this When action is not done as yajña, it becomes ordinary ego-centered karma. The issue is not merely what action is done, but why and how it is done.
Step 3: This world becomes bound
Phrase: loko’yaṁ karmabandhanaḥ = this world is bound by karma Human beings become bound by action when action is performed without the offering attitude. Action done for ego, attachment, and selfish gain creates bondage.
Step 4: Therefore act for that
Phrase: tadarthaṁ karma = action for that sake The “that” refers to yajña. Therefore, action should be performed for yajña — as worship, as contribution, and as alignment with Bhagavān’s order.
Step 5: Address to Arjuna
Phrase: kaunteya = O son of Kuntī Bhagavān directly addresses Arjuna. The instruction is not abstract. Arjuna’s own duty must be converted into yajña. His fighting must not be for hatred or personal gain; it must be performed as dharma.
Step 6: Freedom from attachment
Phrase: muktasaṅgaḥ = free from attachment One should act without clinging to ego, result, praise, possession, or personal agenda. This does not mean careless action. It means careful action without inner dependence.
Step 7: Perform properly
Phrase: samācara = perform well, practice properly Bhagavān does not say merely “do.” He says samācara — perform properly and completely. Karma-yoga requires attention, dharma, devotion, and the right attitude.
Step 8: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse gives the transforming principle of karma-yoga: action plus devotion becomes yajña and liberates; action minus devotion becomes bondage. Therefore, every duty should be converted into worship.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. King Rantideva Offers Even His Last Water (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Rantideva once went through severe hardship. For many days, he and his family had almost nothing to eat. Their bodies were weak, their hunger was real, and their thirst was painful. At last, a little food and water came to them.
Just as Rantideva was about to eat, a hungry guest arrived. Rantideva did not see the visitor as an interruption. He saw an opportunity to serve. He gave a portion of the food.
Then another guest came. Rantideva gave again.
Then another came. Again he shared.
Each time, the food became less. His family was also hungry, and his own body was exhausted. Finally, only a little water remained. That water was precious because Rantideva was burning with thirst.
At that moment, a suffering person came and asked for water.
Rantideva gave even that last water. His prayer was not, “Let me be praised for charity.” His prayer was that the suffering of others may be relieved. His heart had expanded beyond the small boundary of “me and mine.”
This is yajña. His food, water, body, and comfort were not held tightly as personal possessions. They became offerings in the service of Bhagavān present in all beings. His action was not ordinary charity for name or recognition; it was īśvarārpaṇa expressed as compassion.
This story beautifully illustrates 3.9. Action done only for oneself binds. Action done as offering and contribution purifies. Rantideva’s giving was not loss; it was worship.

2. Śabarī Offers Berries to Rāma (Rāmāyaṇa)
Śabarī lived in the forest āśrama of her guru, waiting for Śrī Rāma. Her life was simple. She did not have wealth, royal power, or grand offerings. But she had devotion, patience, and readiness to serve.
Every day she cleaned the āśrama path. Every day she gathered fruits. Every day she waited with the faith that Rāma would come. Years passed, but her devotion did not become tired. Her daily work itself became worship.
At last, Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa arrived. Śabarī’s joy overflowed. She wanted to offer fruits to Rāma, but her love made her careful. She tasted each berry first to make sure it was sweet. Then she offered the sweet ones to Rāma.
From the standpoint of outer ritual, this might appear improper. But Rāma saw the heart. He accepted the berries with delight because they were offered with pure devotion. The value of the offering was not in the fruit; it was in the bhāva.
This story illumines yajña. A small act becomes sacred when it is done for Bhagavān. Śabarī’s cleaning, waiting, gathering, tasting, and offering were all karma-yoga because they were filled with devotion and free from self-importance.
This is the teaching of 3.9: action becomes liberating when it is yajñārtha — done as offering to Bhagavān.

3. Naciketas Questions a Faulty Sacrifice (Kaṭha Upaniṣad)
Vājaśravas was performing a sacrifice in which he was to give away his possessions. Outwardly, the ritual was grand. Sacred fire was present. Gifts were being distributed. The form of yajña was visible.
But young Naciketas noticed something troubling. The cows being given away were old, weak, and no longer useful. They had drunk their last water, eaten their last grass, and given their last milk. The outer act was called charity, but the inner spirit did not match the sacred form.
Naciketas was disturbed because he understood something subtle: yajña is not merely external performance. It requires sincerity. If one gives away only what is useless, while keeping the best for oneself, the action may look religious but it is not true offering.
So Naciketas asked his father, “To whom will you give me?” He asked again and again, not to insult his father, but because he was sensitive to the truth of sacrifice. His question exposed the gap between ritual action and genuine yajña-bhāva.
This story is very important for 3.9. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says that action done for yajña liberates, but action done otherwise binds. Naciketas shows that even a ritual called yajña can lose its spirit if it lacks sincerity, offering, and inner alignment.
The lesson is clear: the outer action alone is not enough. Karma becomes yajña only when it is performed with honesty, reverence, and freedom from selfish calculation.

4. Mahābali Offers Himself to Vāmana (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Mahābali was powerful, generous, and celebrated. When Vāmana, the dwarf brāhmaṇa, came to his sacrifice, Bali received him respectfully and asked what he desired. Vāmana asked for only three steps of land.
Bali smiled. Such a small request seemed easy for a king who could give much more. But Śukrācārya warned him: this was no ordinary brāhmaṇa. This was Bhagavān Viṣṇu himself. If Bali gave the promise, he would lose everything.
Now Bali faced a test. Was his giving only for fame, or was it true offering? Was yajña merely a public display, or was he ready to surrender what he called “mine”?
Bali kept his word. Vāmana grew into Trivikrama, the cosmic form. With one step he covered the earth. With another he covered the heavens. There was no place left for the third step.
Then Bali bowed his head and offered himself.
This is one of the deepest forms of yajña. Bali’s offering moved from land, wealth, and kingdom to the surrender of ego itself. He did not merely give an object; he gave up possessiveness. Even when bound, he became inwardly great because his action became self-offering to Bhagavān.
This story connects powerfully to 3.9. Yajña is not just giving things. It is releasing the tight claim of “mine.” Action done with surrender liberates; action done with possessiveness binds.

5. Bhagīratha Brings Gaṅgā for His Ancestors and the World (Rāmāyaṇa / Purāṇic Tradition)
King Bhagīratha carried a sacred responsibility. His ancestors, the sons of Sagara, needed liberation, and only the descent of Gaṅgā could bless them. This was not a small personal task. It required immense tapas, patience, and dedication.
Bhagīratha did not say, “This is too difficult.” He did not abandon the duty because the result seemed impossible. He performed severe tapas to bring Gaṅgā from the heavens to the earth. When Gaṅgā agreed to descend, another problem arose: her force would shatter the earth. Bhagīratha then worshipped Śiva, who received Gaṅgā in his matted locks and released her gently.
Bhagīratha continued to guide the river until she reached the ashes of his ancestors and blessed them. But the result of his effort did not stop with his family. Gaṅgā became a blessing for the world — nourishing lands, sanctifying people, supporting worship, and becoming a sacred river for generations.
This story shows action performed for a purpose larger than oneself. Bhagīratha’s tapas was not for personal pleasure or fame. It was for duty, ancestors, and the welfare of the world. His action became yajña because it connected personal responsibility with cosmic blessing.
This is the spirit of 3.9. When action is done only for personal gain, it binds. When action is done as sacred contribution, aligned with dharma and Bhagavān’s order, it purifies and uplifts.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: यज्ञार्थात् कर्मणः अन्यत्र लोकः अयम् कर्मबन्धनः । तदर्थम् कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः समाचर ॥३.९॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: यज्ञार्थात् = for the sake of yajña / other than action meant for yajña | कर्मणः = from action / than action | अन्यत्र = otherwise, apart from | लोकः = the world, people | अयम् = this | कर्मबन्धनः = bound by action | तदर्थम् = for that sake | कर्म = action | कौन्तेय = O son of Kuntī | मुक्तसङ्गः = free from attachment | समाचर = perform properly
rich_grammar.anvayah: हे कौन्तेय, यज्ञार्थात् कर्मणः अन्यत्र अयं लोकः कर्मबन्धनः । तदर्थं मुक्तसङ्गः कर्म समाचर ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: यज्ञ + अर्थात् = यज्ञार्थात् | कर्मणः + अन्यत्र = कर्मणोऽन्यत्र | लोकः + अयम् = लोकोऽयम् | तत् + अर्थम् = तदर्थम् | मुक्त + सङ्गः = मुक्तसङ्गः
rich_grammar.samasa: यज्ञार्थात् = यज्ञस्य अर्थः / यज्ञार्थः Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; for the sake of yajña. | कर्मबन्धनः = कर्मणा बन्धनम् / कर्मबन्धनयुक्तः Samāsa name: तृतीया तत्पुरुष sense; bound by action. In the verse it functions adjectivally with लोकः. | तदर्थम् = तस्य अर्थम् Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; for that sake. | मुक्तसङ्गः = मुक्तः सङ्गः यस्य सः Samāsa name: बहुव्रीहि समासः; one whose attachment is released, free from attachment.
rich_grammar.other: अन्यत्र is an indeclinable meaning “elsewhere,” “otherwise,” or “apart from.” | कर्मणः is पञ्चमी विभक्ति, used with अन्यत्र to indicate “apart from action done for yajña.” | लोकः अयम् together means “this world” or “this human world.” In teaching context, it refers to people who become bound by action. | कर्मबन्धनः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग, agreeing with लोकः. | तदर्थम् functions adverbially: “for that sake,” referring back to yajña. | कर्म is द्वितीया एकवचनम्, the object of समाचर. | समाचर is लोट्-लकारः, a direct imperative: “perform properly.” | मुक्तसङ्गः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग, qualifying the implied subject “you,” Arjuna.

key_verses.final_takeaway: When action is performed as an offering to the Lord — as yajña — it liberates. When done with selfish attachment, it binds. The key is not what we do, but the spirit in which we do it: with gratitude, humility, and surrender.

key_verses.title: Creation and the Gift of Yajña
key_verses.sanskrit:
सहयज्ञाः(फ्) प्रजाः(स्) सृष्ट्वा पुरोवाच प्रजापतिः ।
अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वम् एष वोऽस्त्विष्टकामधुक् ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
sahayajñāḥ(f) prajāḥ(s) sṛṣṭvā purovāca prajāpatiḥ ।
anena prasaviṣyadhvam eṣa vo’stviṣṭakāmadhuk ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Having created human beings along with yajña in the beginning, Prajāpati said: “May you prosper by this yajña; may this be the source of your desired blessings.”
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now explains that the yajña way of life is not a later human invention. It is part of the original design of creation. Prajāpati, the Creator, created human beings along with yajña and instructed them to prosper through it.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now explains that the yajña way of life is not a later human invention. It is part of the original design of creation. Prajāpati, the Creator, created human beings along with yajña and instructed them to prosper through it.

Here yajña means a life of sacrifice, contribution, sharing, reverence, and worshipful action. Human beings are not meant to live as isolated consumers. We are born into a vast network of support: nature, society, parents, teachers, animals, plants, rivers, rain, food, and the unseen order of Bhagavān. Since we receive from the whole, we must contribute back to the whole.

This is why the Veda is compared to a manual that comes along with creation. When a manufacturer gives a product, the manufacturer also gives instructions for proper use. Those instructions are not for the manufacturer’s benefit; they are for the user’s benefit. Similarly, Bhagavān gives the Veda as the guide for human life. It tells us how to live in harmony with the world and how to make our life spiritually meaningful.

The verse says: anena prasaviṣyadhvam — may you prosper by this. Prosperity here is not merely money or comfort. It includes outer well-being, social harmony, inner maturity, dhārmic living, and preparation for mokṣa. A yajña life creates harmony; a selfish life creates disorder.

The verse also says: eṣa vo’stviṣṭakāmadhuk — may this yajña be the giver of desired blessings. Like a wish-fulfilling cow, yajña nourishes life. When human beings live with contribution and reverence, the world becomes supportive. When we only take and do not give, the cycle is disturbed.

Therefore, 3.10 teaches that karma-yoga is not just personal discipline. It is the way human beings participate responsibly in creation.
key_verses.story:
King Pṛthu Restores the Earth’s Abundance (Bhāgavata Purāṇa / Viṣṇu Purāṇa)
There was a time when the earth withheld her abundance. Crops did not flourish properly, and people suffered. King Pṛthu saw that his subjects were in distress. As a king, he could not live for himself alone. His duty was to protect, nourish, and guide the people.
The earth had taken the form of a cow and was not freely yielding her resources. Pṛthu pursued her, not out of personal greed, but because the welfare of the people was at stake. The earth then explained that if human beings act without responsibility, she cannot continue to give endlessly. Resources must be received with reverence and used properly.
Pṛthu did not respond as an exploiter. He established order. He made arrangements so that the earth could be “milked” properly — not violently, not selfishly, but according to dharma. Different beings received what was appropriate for them. Agriculture, nourishment, and social order were restored.
This story beautifully illustrates 3.10. Creation is not meant for blind consumption. The earth is like a nourishing cow, but she yields blessings when human beings live with responsibility, reverence, and yajña-bhāva. If we take without contributing, disorder comes. If we live according to the divine manual of dharma, prosperity becomes possible.
Prajāpati’s instruction — “May you prosper by yajña” — is seen here in action. Pṛthu’s kingship becomes yajña because he restores harmony between human beings and the earth.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka teaches that human life is not designed for consumption alone. We are born into a yajña-system. Every day we receive before we give. We wake up breathing air we did not create. We drink water we did not produce. We eat food that came through soil, rain, farmers, transporters, shopkeepers, and cooks. We use language, roads, temples, schools, books, and traditions built by others.

A mature life asks: “How do I complete the cycle?” If I only take, I become a burden on the order. If I contribute, I participate in yajña.

In Indian life, this can be practiced very concretely. Feeding guests, giving dānam, doing annadānam, caring for parents, teaching children, paying workers fairly, protecting rivers and trees, avoiding waste, supporting temples and gurukulas, doing one’s profession honestly, and praying before eating are all ways of living yajña.

This shloka also corrects our idea of prosperity. Prosperity is not merely more possessions. Real prosperity is a life supported by harmony — harmony with family, society, nature, dharma, and Bhagavān. A society that forgets yajña may become rich in objects but poor in peace. A person who lives yajña may have simple possessions but a rich heart.

The practical message is: treat life as received grace, not private ownership. Take gratefully. Use responsibly. Give back generously. That is the Creator’s manual for human prosperity.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I living mainly as a consumer, or am I consciously contributing back to the order that supports me?
Which part of my life can become more yajña-based: food, money, work, family, nature, or study?
What is one blessing I receive daily that I can repay through service, reverence, or sharing?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says that Bhagavān made people with a special rule: “Do not only take. Also give.”
We get so many things every day. We get food, water, air, sunlight, parents, teachers, friends, books, and a home. We did not make all these by ourselves.
So we should also give back. We can help at home. We can share food. We can water plants. We can keep our room clean. We can say thank you before eating. We can be kind to people who help us.
This is called yajña. It means living with a thankful heart and helping attitude. When we live like this, life becomes happy and blessed.
kids_content.story:
Meera and the School Garden
Meera’s school had a small garden near the playground. There were tulasī plants, jasmine flowers, and two young mango trees. Every day, the children played near it, enjoyed the shade, and sometimes smelled the flowers.
One afternoon, the teacher said, “Children, the garden gives us beauty and shade. Today we will give something back.”
Each child received a small job. One child pulled weeds. Another picked up dry leaves. Another filled water cans. Meera was asked to water the tulasī plants.
At first, Meera thought, “But I want to play.” Then she looked at the plants. Their leaves were slightly drooping in the hot sun. She carefully poured water near the roots.
The next week, the jasmine plant had more flowers. The mango leaves looked brighter. The playground felt more beautiful.
The teacher said, “When we enjoy the garden, we should also care for it. That is how life works. We receive, and we give back.”
Meera understood. The garden was not just “there for her.” She was also responsible for it. Kṛṣṇa teaches the same thing: the world gives us so much, and we should live with gratitude and contribution.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one thing I receive every day that I should say thank you for?
What is one small way I can give back today?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Human beings created with yajña
Phrase: sahayajñāḥ prajāḥ sṛṣṭvā = having created beings along with yajña Human beings were created together with yajña. This means yajña is not an optional extra. A life of contribution, reverence, and offering belongs to the very design of human life.
Step 2: In the beginning
Phrase: purā = in the beginning The teaching is ancient and original. From the beginning of creation, human beings were given a way of life that would preserve harmony and support prosperity.
Step 3: Prajāpati instructed
Phrase: uvāca prajāpatiḥ = Prajāpati said Prajāpati, the Creator, gives instruction to human beings. This connects to the idea that the Veda is the manual of life, given by Bhagavān for the benefit of the created beings.
Step 4: Prosper by this
Phrase: anena prasaviṣyadhvam = may you prosper by this “By this” means by yajña. Prosperity comes through a life of sacrifice, sharing, contribution, and harmony. This prosperity includes material support, social well-being, mental refinement, and spiritual growth.
Step 5: This is your source of desired blessings
Phrase: eṣa vaḥ astu iṣṭakāmadhuk = may this be the giver of your desired objects Yajña is compared to a wish-fulfilling source. When human beings live in harmony with the order, the order supports them. The world becomes nourishing when life is lived reverentially.
Step 6: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse teaches that karma-yoga is woven into creation. Human prosperity depends on yajña. Therefore, action should not be selfish consumption; it should be contribution aligned with Bhagavān’s order.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. King Janaka Ploughs the Field and Receives Sītā (Rāmāyaṇa Tradition)
King Janaka was a ruler, but he was not separated from the earth that nourished his people. He understood that prosperity does not come merely from royal power. It comes from dharma, rain, soil, seed, effort, and Bhagavān’s grace.
During a sacred agricultural rite, Janaka himself entered the field with a plough. This was not ordinary farming. It was a yajña-like action — a king approaching the earth with reverence, humility, and responsibility. The field was prepared, the atmosphere was sacred, and the king placed his own hands on the plough.
As he ploughed, the blade touched something hidden in the earth. The ground opened, and there appeared a divine child. Janaka was filled with wonder. This was not the fruit of personal ambition. It was a blessing arising from the earth herself.
He received the child with reverence and named her Sītā, because she was found in the furrow made by the plough. He raised her as his daughter, not as a possession, but as a sacred gift.
This story reflects the spirit of 3.10. Prajāpati says that beings were created together with yajña and should prosper through yajña. Janaka’s action shows that when human effort is joined with reverence for the earth and alignment with dharma, prosperity becomes sacred. The earth is not an object to exploit; she is a source of blessing when approached with yajña-bhāva.

2. Kṛṣṇa Redirects the Vraja Yajña to Govardhana (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
In Vraja, the cowherds were preparing a sacrifice for Indra. Their hope was simple: may Indra give rain, may the grass grow, may the cows be nourished, and may the village prosper.
Young Kṛṣṇa watched the preparations and asked a question. Why were they worshipping Indra mechanically? Was it not Govardhana hill that gave grass? Was it not the cows that sustained their life? Was it not the land, the forests, the brāhmaṇas, and the community that directly supported them?
Kṛṣṇa was not rejecting gratitude. He was purifying it. He taught them to honor the visible forms through which Bhagavān’s order nourished them. The people of Vraja then offered food, flowers, and worship to Govardhana, the cows, and the sacred order around them.
Indra became angry and sent terrible rain. The clouds roared, the wind lashed, and the frightened people ran to Kṛṣṇa. Kṛṣṇa lifted Govardhana hill like an umbrella and sheltered the entire community beneath it — people, cows, carts, children, elders, and all.
The storm ended. Indra’s pride was humbled. Vraja learned that yajña is not mechanical ritual for selfish gain. True yajña is reverence for Bhagavān’s sustaining order. This story fits 3.10 beautifully: through yajña, human beings prosper because they live in gratitude, harmony, and sacred dependence on the whole.

3. Ṛśyaśṛṅga Brings Rain to Aṅga (Rāmāyaṇa)
The kingdom of Aṅga was suffering. The skies had become empty. Fields cracked under the heat. Crops failed. Cattle weakened. People looked upward, but no rain came. The drought was not merely a weather problem; it was felt as a breakdown in dhārmic harmony.
King Romapāda was troubled. A king’s duty is not only to sit on a throne, but to protect the well-being of the people. When the land suffers, the king must look inward as well as outward. The elders and priests understood that the kingdom needed the presence of purity, tapas, and sacred order.
Ṛśyaśṛṅga, a sage of great innocence and spiritual power, was brought to Aṅga. His arrival itself changed the atmosphere. The people watched with hope. The parched land waited. The king received him with reverence, and the lost connection with dharma began to be restored.
Then the rains came.
The dry earth softened. The smell of wet soil rose. Fields revived. Seeds could grow. Cattle could graze. People who had lived in fear now saw nourishment returning.
This story illustrates 3.10 because Prajāpati’s teaching links yajña with prosperity. Human life, nature, rain, food, dharma, and sacred living are not separate. When life is aligned with yajña — reverence, purity, duty, and contribution — the world becomes nourishing again.

4. The Churning of the Ocean Produces Amṛta (Bhāgavata Purāṇa / Purāṇic Tradition)
The devas had lost their strength and needed amṛta, the nectar of immortality. But amṛta was hidden in the ocean of milk. It could not be obtained by wishful thinking or by one person’s effort. A vast yajña-like undertaking was required.
Mount Mandara was brought as the churning rod. Vāsuki became the rope. The devas stood on one side and the asuras on the other. They pulled with great effort. The ocean roared. The mountain shook. Bhagavān himself supported the mountain as Kūrma, the divine tortoise, so the work could continue.
But the first result was not nectar. Poison emerged. The terrifying hālāhala threatened all beings. Śiva received the poison and protected creation. Only after this danger was faced did treasures begin to arise: divine beings, jewels, blessings, and finally amṛta.
This story is a cosmic picture of yajña. Everyone contributes. The work is difficult. Impurities may surface first. Divine support is needed. But when effort is aligned with a higher purpose, blessings emerge.
The shloka says yajña becomes iṣṭakāmadhuk, the source of desired blessings. The churning of the ocean shows this on a grand scale. Prosperity is not produced by selfish grabbing; it comes through disciplined cooperation, sacrifice, divine alignment, and perseverance.

5. Annapūrṇā Feeds the World (Purāṇic / Śākta Tradition)
In Kāśī, the sacred city of light, Devī appeared as Annapūrṇā, the giver of food. The image is deeply meaningful: Bhagavān Śiva himself comes as a bhikṣuka, a mendicant, and Devī stands with a vessel and ladle, ready to feed.
The scene teaches a truth that is easy to forget. Even the highest seeker lives in a body. The body depends on food. Food depends on earth, water, sunlight, time, labor, and Bhagavān’s order. Food is not merely an object on a plate. It is grace made visible.
Devī Annapūrṇā feeds not with pride, but with motherly abundance. Śiva receives, showing that receiving food with humility is also sacred. The hungry are nourished. The seeker is nourished. The world is nourished.
When food is cooked with purity, offered to Bhagavān, shared with others, and received with gratitude, the kitchen becomes a yajñaśālā. Feeding becomes worship. Eating becomes worship. Sharing becomes worship.
This story connects directly to 3.10. Prajāpati says that through yajña, beings may prosper. Food is one of the most immediate forms of prosperity. Annapūrṇā reminds us that nourishment flows when life is lived with reverence, gratitude, and offering.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: सहयज्ञाः प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा पुरा उवाच प्रजापतिः । अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वम् एषः वः अस्तु इष्टकामधुक् ॥३.१०॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: सहयज्ञाः = together with yajña | प्रजाः = beings, human beings | सृष्ट्वा = having created | पुरा = in the beginning, long ago | उवाच = said | प्रजापतिः = Prajāpati, the Creator | अनेन = by this | प्रसविष्यध्वम् = may you prosper, may you multiply | एषः = this | वः = for you / your | अस्तु = may it be | इष्टकामधुक् = giver of desired objects
rich_grammar.anvayah: प्रजापतिः पुरा सहयज्ञाः प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा उवाच — अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वम् । एषः वः इष्टकामधुक् अस्तु ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: पुरा + उवाच = पुरोवाच | प्रसविष्यध्वम् + एषः = प्रसविष्यध्वमेषः | एषः + वः = एष | वः + अस्तु = वोऽस्तु | अस्तु + इष्टकामधुक् = अस्त्विष्टकामधुक्
rich_grammar.samasa: सहयज्ञाः = यज्ञेन सह वर्तमानाः Samāsa sense: बहुव्रीहि-like adjectival expression; those who are created together with yajña. It qualifies प्रजाः. | प्रजापतिः = प्रजानां पतिः Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; lord or creator of beings. | इष्टकामधुक् = इष्टान् कामान् दुहति इति Samāsa sense: बहुव्रीहि / उपपद-type expression; that which yields desired objects, like a wish-fulfilling cow.
rich_grammar.other: सृष्ट्वा is a क्त्वान्त-कृदन्तम् from सृज्, meaning “having created.” | उवाच is लिट्-लकारः from वच्, meaning “said.” | अनेन is तृतीया एकवचनम्, meaning “by this,” referring to yajña. | प्रसविष्यध्वम् is a future form in मध्यमपुरुष बहुवचनम्, addressed to the created beings: “may you prosper” or “you shall prosper.” | अस्तु is लोट्-लकारः from अस्, expressing blessing or command: “may it be.” | वः can be understood as “for you” or “your,” depending on connection: “may this be for you the giver of desired objects.” | इष्टकामधुक् is प्रथमा एकवचनम्, agreeing with एषः.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Yajña is not a later invention but part of the original design of creation. Bhagavān gave human beings duties and yajña together, so that through contributing and offering, they may prosper and grow toward the highest good.

key_verses.title: Mutual Nourishment Through Yajña
key_verses.sanskrit:
देवान्भावयतानेन ते देवा भावयन्तु वः ।
परस्परं भावयन्तः(श्) श्रेयः(फ्) परमवाप्स्यथ ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
devānbhāvayatānena te devā bhāvayantu vaḥ ।
parasparaṁ bhāvayantaḥ(sh) śreyaḥ(f) paramavāpsyatha ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Nourish the devatās through yajña, and may those devatās nourish you; thus, mutually nourishing one another, you shall attain the supreme good.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the teaching of yajña. In the previous shloka, Prajāpati created human beings along with yajña and instructed them to prosper through it. Now the verse explains how that prosperity happens: through mutual nourishment between human beings and the devatās.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the teaching of yajña. In the previous shloka, Prajāpati created human beings along with yajña and instructed them to prosper through it. Now the verse explains how that prosperity happens: through mutual nourishment between human beings and the devatās.

Here yajña must be understood as karma-yoga itself — a life of sacrifice, contribution, sharing, worship, and harmony. It is not merely a fire ritual. A yajña-life means that I do not live only as a consumer. I recognize that I am constantly receiving from the whole: from nature, society, parents, teachers, scriptures, animals, plants, rivers, rain, sunlight, and Bhagavān’s order. Therefore, I live by giving back.

This giving back is expressed through the pañca-mahā-yajñas: reverence and worship of the devatās, gratitude to ancestors, study and preservation of scripture, service to human beings, and care for animals, plants, and the environment. A person living such a life does not exploit nature to feed greed. He lives with restraint, gratitude, and responsibility.

The devatās are the presiding deities of natural forces. Indra presides over rain and thunder. Varuṇa presides over waters. Agni presides over fire. Sūrya gives light and energy. These devatās represent the sacred order behind nature. To nourish the devatās means to live in harmony with this order through yajña, dharma, worship, contribution, and non-exploitation.

Then the devatās nourish us in return. Rain comes properly. Rivers flow. Crops grow. Fire cooks. Sunlight sustains life. Seasons move in rhythm. Society receives the conditions needed for health, food, livelihood, and spiritual pursuit.

This mutual nourishment is called parasparaṁ bhāvayantaḥ — nourishing one another. Human beings support the cosmic order through yajña, and the cosmic order supports human beings through natural abundance. When this relationship is broken, life becomes conflict. When it is honored, there is prosperity and progress.

The verse ends with śreyaḥ param avāpsyatha — you will attain the supreme good. This includes not merely material prosperity but also inner maturity, dhārmic living, and readiness for mokṣa. Therefore, yajña is ecological, social, religious, and spiritual responsibility all together.
key_verses.story:
Kṛṣṇa Lifts Govardhana and Teaches Reverence for Nature (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
In Vraja, the cowherds were preparing their annual worship of Indra. Their thought was simple: Indra gives rain, rain gives grass, grass nourishes cows, and cows sustain the village. So the people prepared offerings, food, and ritual arrangements.
Young Kṛṣṇa asked them to think more deeply. “Who directly supports your life? Is it not Govardhana hill, the cows, the forests, the pastures, the brāhmaṇas, and the land around you?” He was not teaching disrespect toward the sacred order. He was teaching them to recognize that Bhagavān’s order nourishes us through the immediate world around us.
The people accepted Kṛṣṇa’s teaching. They worshipped Govardhana, honored the cows, fed the brāhmaṇas, and celebrated the land that sustained them. Food was offered in abundance. The whole community participated in gratitude.
Indra became angry. Feeling ignored, he sent terrible rain. Clouds roared. Winds struck. Water poured down in frightening torrents. The people of Vraja ran to Kṛṣṇa for refuge. Then Kṛṣṇa lifted Govardhana hill with one hand and held it like an umbrella. Beneath the hill, cows, calves, children, elders, carts, and families found shelter.
For seven days, the village remained protected. Indra’s pride was humbled. He understood that the divine order is not meant to be controlled by ego. The people of Vraja learned that yajña is not mechanical ritual or fearful appeasement. It is grateful participation in the sacred order that nourishes life.
This story illumines 3.11. Human beings must nourish the devatās through reverence, gratitude, and yajña. The devatās nourish human beings through rain, land, cows, food, and natural harmony. When this relationship becomes egoistic, disorder comes. When it becomes reverential, there is protection, prosperity, and śreyas.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka is very relevant today. Much of modern life treats nature as something to be conquered, consumed, and controlled. We speak of conquering mountains, exploiting resources, maximizing output, and bending nature to human desire. But this verse teaches a different vision: nature is not our enemy. We are meant to live in cooperation with the natural order.

We receive from the devatās every day. Sūrya gives light. Varuṇa gives water. Agni gives heat. Vāyu gives air. Pṛthivī gives food. Trees give oxygen, shade, flowers, fruits, and beauty. Rivers support agriculture and life. Cows, animals, soil, rain, and seasons all participate in our survival.

If we only take and never nourish the order, imbalance follows. Pollution, waste, water misuse, cruelty to animals, food disrespect, and greedy consumption are not merely environmental issues; they show loss of yajña-bhāva. A yajña-life means gratitude and responsible participation.

In Indian life, this can be practiced through simple and sacred actions: praying before meals, not wasting food, protecting water, planting trees, feeding animals responsibly, honoring cows, doing dānam, supporting temples and gurukulas, serving society, and using one’s profession in a dhārmic way.

The verse also applies to social life. Parents nourish children, and children later nourish parents. Teachers nourish students, and students preserve the teaching. Society supports us, and we support society. Mutual nourishment is the law of healthy living.

The practical message is: do not live as a consumer alone. Live as a contributor. Nourish what nourishes you. That is yajña. That is harmony. That becomes the path to śreyas.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I treat nature mainly as something to use, or as a sacred order to respect and nourish?
What nourishes me every day that I may be taking for granted?
How can I consciously nourish back — through gratitude, restraint, service, dānam, or care for nature?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says, “Take care of what takes care of you.”
The sun gives light. Trees give shade and fruits. Plants give flowers and food. Cows give milk. Rivers give water. Parents, teachers, and friends also help us.
So we should not only take. We should also give back.
We can water plants. We can avoid wasting food. We can keep water clean. We can feed birds carefully. We can say thank you before eating. We can help parents and teachers. This is yajña.
When we care for the world, the world cares for us. That is what Kṛṣṇa is teaching Arjuna.
kids_content.story:
Kavya and the Mango Tree
Kavya’s house had a mango tree in the backyard. In summer, the tree gave sweet mangoes. In the afternoon, it gave shade. Birds sat on its branches and sang.
Kavya loved eating the mangoes. But she never thought much about the tree. One hot day, she saw the leaves looking dusty and tired. The soil near the roots was dry.
Her grandfather said, “Kavya, this tree gives us so much. Shall we give something back?”
Kavya brought a small bucket of water. She poured it near the roots. Grandfather showed her how to place dry leaves around the soil so it would stay cool. They picked up plastic wrappers that had blown into the yard.
A few days later, the leaves looked brighter. A little bird came and sat on a branch. Kavya smiled and said, “The tree looks happy.”
Grandfather said, “When we care for what cares for us, life becomes beautiful.”
From that day, Kavya did not only run to the tree for mangoes. She watered it, protected it, and thanked it. She learned Kṛṣṇa’s teaching: nourish what nourishes you.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one thing in nature that helps me every day?
What is one small way I can take care of it today?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Nourish the devatās
Phrase: devān bhāvayata anena = nourish the devatās by this Anena means “by this yajña.” Human beings nourish the devatās by living a yajña-life — worship, gratitude, dharma, restraint, and contribution. The devatās are the presiding deities of natural forces.
Step 2: The devatās nourish you
Phrase: te devā bhāvayantu vaḥ = may those devatās nourish you When the devatās are honored through yajña, they nourish human beings through rain, food, water, fire, sunlight, seasons, and natural harmony. This is not a commercial bargain; it is participation in the sacred order.
Step 3: Mutual nourishment
Phrase: parasparaṁ bhāvayantaḥ = nourishing one another The relationship between human beings and the cosmic order is reciprocal. We receive and we give. We are supported and we support. This is the yajña-cycle.
Step 4: Attaining śreyas
Phrase: śreyaḥ param avāpsyatha = you shall attain supreme good The result of mutual nourishment is param śreyas, the highest good. At one level, it includes social and ecological harmony. At a deeper level, it creates the inner maturity needed for mokṣa.
Step 5: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse teaches cooperation with nature, not conquest of nature. Karma-yoga is a life of harmony with Bhagavān’s order. Human beings, devatās, nature, and society are meant to support one another in yajña.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. King Daśaratha Performs Putrakāmeṣṭi Yajña (Rāmāyaṇa)
Ayodhyā was prosperous, but King Daśaratha carried a deep sorrow: he had no child to continue the lineage. This was not merely a private concern. A king’s heir meant continuity of dharma, stability for the people, and protection of the kingdom.
The sages advised him to perform the Putrakāmeṣṭi yajña. Ṛśyaśṛṅga was invited to conduct the sacred rite. The yajña was prepared with care. The fire was kindled. Mantras were chanted. The king, queens, priests, and citizens participated with reverence.
This was not a mechanical ritual or a bargain with the devatās. It was sacred alignment with Bhagavān’s order. Human beings offered with faith, discipline, and humility. Through yajña, they acknowledged that life, children, prosperity, and continuity do not come merely by human will.
From the fire emerged a divine being carrying pāyasa, the sacred sweet offering. Daśaratha received it with devotion and gave it to his queens. In time, Rāma, Bharata, Lakṣmaṇa, and Śatrughna were born.
This story illustrates 3.11 beautifully. Human beings nourished the devatās through yajña, and the devatās nourished the kingdom in return through divine blessing. Mutual nourishment produced not only personal joy but dhārmic prosperity for the whole kingdom.

2. Bhagīratha Brings Gaṅgā to Earth (Rāmāyaṇa / Purāṇic Tradition)
King Bhagīratha carried a sacred responsibility. His ancestors, the sons of Sagara, had been reduced to ashes and needed the purifying touch of Gaṅgā to attain blessing. But Gaṅgā flowed in the heavens. Bringing her to earth seemed impossible.
Bhagīratha did not give up. He performed severe tapas with one-pointed determination. His effort was not for personal pleasure or fame. It was for his ancestors, for dharma, and ultimately for the world.
Gaṅgā agreed to descend, but another danger appeared. Her force was so great that the earth could not bear it. Bhagīratha then worshipped Śiva. Śiva received Gaṅgā in his matted locks and released her gently, making her descent safe.
Bhagīratha then guided the river across the earth until she reached the ashes of his ancestors and blessed them. But her blessing did not stop there. Gaṅgā became a sacred river for generations — nourishing fields, purifying pilgrims, supporting worship, and sustaining life.
This story is a powerful example of mutual nourishment. Bhagīratha’s tapas and dhārmic effort invoked divine and natural forces. Śiva, Gaṅgā, earth, ancestors, and human beings all became part of one sacred cycle. This is the spirit of 3.11: when human beings live with yajña-bhāva, the devatās and nature nourish the world in return.

3. Ṛśyaśṛṅga Brings Rain to Aṅga (Rāmāyaṇa)
The kingdom of Aṅga was suffering from drought. The skies had become empty. Fields cracked under the heat. Crops failed. Cattle weakened. People looked upward, but no rain came.
King Romapāda was deeply troubled. A king’s duty is not only to rule but to protect the well-being of the people. When the land suffers, the king must restore dharma, not merely search for external fixes.
Ṛśyaśṛṅga, a sage of great purity and tapas, was brought to the kingdom. The people watched with hope. The king received him with reverence. The presence of purity, yajña, and sacred order returned to the land.
Then the rains came.
The dry earth softened. The smell of wet soil rose. Seeds awakened. Cattle could graze. People who had lived in fear saw nourishment returning.
This story fits 3.11 directly. The devatās nourish through rain, but human beings must live in harmony with dharma and yajña. When that harmony is restored, nature becomes supportive again. Mutual nourishment brings prosperity.

4. Agni Carries the Offerings to the Devas (Vedic / Purāṇic Tradition)
In Vedic yajña, Agni is not seen as mere physical fire. Agni is the divine messenger who carries offerings to the devatās. When the yajña fire is lit, the sacrificer does not think, “I am burning things.” He thinks, “I am offering.”
Ghee, grains, herbs, and mantras are offered into the fire. The flames rise upward. Agni receives the offerings and carries them to the devas. The devas are pleased, and the cosmic order is nourished.
In return, human beings receive rain, food, health, clarity, and the conditions needed for dharma. A small fire on the altar becomes a bridge between the human and cosmic levels.
This story-like Vedic vision expresses the heart of 3.11. Human beings nourish the devatās through yajña. The devatās nourish human beings through the forces of nature. Agni stands as the sacred link between offering and blessing.

5. King Yudhiṣṭhira’s Rājasūya Honors the Whole Order (Mahābhārata)
When Yudhiṣṭhira performed the Rājasūya yajña, it was not meant to be a private royal celebration. It was a sacred act involving the whole order of society. Kings, sages, elders, brāhmaṇas, warriors, guests, servants, and citizens all had their place.
The fires were worshipped. Guests were honored. Gifts were given. Food was distributed. The learned were respected. The poor and dependent were cared for. The king did not stand alone as an enjoyer of power; he stood as one who had received from the whole and now gave back to the whole.
Yudhiṣṭhira’s greatness was in recognizing that rulership itself must be yajña. A king receives support from land, people, army, elders, sages, devatās, and Bhagavān’s order. Therefore, he must nourish all of them through protection, generosity, justice, and worship.
This story connects to 3.11 because society itself must be built on reciprocity. Rulers nourish people. People support dharma. Sages guide society. Devatās sustain nature. Nature sustains life. When each nourishes the other, there is prosperity and śreyas.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: देवान् भावयत अनेन ते देवाः भावयन्तु वः । परस्परम् भावयन्तः श्रेयः परम् अवाप्स्यथ ॥३.११॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: देवान् = the devatās | भावयत = nourish, please, sustain | अनेन = by this, through this yajña | ते = those | देवाः = devatās | भावयन्तु = may they nourish | वः = you | परस्परम् = one another, mutually | भावयन्तः = nourishing | श्रेयः = good, welfare | परम् = supreme | अवाप्स्यथ = you shall attain
rich_grammar.anvayah: अनेन देवान् भावयत । ते देवाः वः भावयन्तु । परस्परं भावयन्तः परं श्रेयः अवाप्स्यथ ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: देवान् + भावयत = देवान्भावयत | भावयत + अनेन = भावयतानेन | देवाः + भावयन्तु = देवा | वः + परस्परम् = वः | परम् + अवाप्स्यथ = परमवाप्स्यथ
rich_grammar.samasa: There is no major compound central to the syntax of this shloka. | परस्परम् is used as an indeclinable meaning “mutually” or “one another.”
rich_grammar.other: भावयत and भावयन्तु are causative forms from भू with णिच्, meaning “cause to thrive,” “nourish,” “please,” or “sustain.” | भावयत is लोट्-लकारः, मध्यमपुरुष बहुवचनम्: “may you nourish” or “nourish.” | भावयन्तु is लोट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम्: “may they nourish.” | भावयन्तः is a present active participle, प्रथमा बहुवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग, referring to those mutually nourishing one another. | अनेन is तृतीया विभक्ति: “by this,” referring to yajña from the previous context. | वः is a second-person plural pronoun, here meaning “you” or “for you.” | श्रेयः परम् together means “supreme good.” परम् qualifies श्रेयः. | अवाप्स्यथ is future tense, मध्यमपुरुष बहुवचनम्: “you shall attain.”

key_verses.final_takeaway: Life is sustained by mutual giving. When human beings serve the cosmic order through yajña, the order sustains them in return. This reciprocity — between the individual and the whole — leads to the highest good.
