key_verses.title: Enjoyment Without Gratitude Is Theft
key_verses.sanskrit:
इष्टान्भोगान्हि वो देवा(:) दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः ।
तैर्दत्तानप्रदायैभ्यो(यः) यो भुङ्क्ते स्तेन एव सः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
iṣṭānbhogānhi vo devā(ḥ) dāsyante yajñabhāvitāḥ ।
tairdattānapradāyaibhyo(aḥ) yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: The devatās, nourished by yajña, give desired enjoyments; one who enjoys their gifts without offering back is indeed a thief.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the yajña teaching. In the previous shloka, human beings were asked to nourish the devatās through yajña, and the devatās were said to nourish human beings in return. Now this shloka explains the responsibility that comes with receiving.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the yajña teaching. In the previous shloka, human beings were asked to nourish the devatās through yajña, and the devatās were said to nourish human beings in return. Now this shloka explains the responsibility that comes with receiving.

The devatās are nourished by yajña. Here yajña means karma-yoga — a life of reverence, offering, gratitude, dharma, and non-exploitation. When we live in harmony with Bhagavān’s order, we do not exploit nature merely to feed greed. We use the world carefully, reverentially, and responsibly. That yajña-life nourishes the devatās, the presiding deities of natural forces.

The devatās then bless us with iṣṭān bhogān — desired enjoyments and supports of life. These include food, water, clothing, shelter, health, rain, sunlight, fire, air, prosperity, family support, social order, and the conditions for pursuing dharma, artha, kāma, and mokṣa.

These blessings do not come from human effort alone. A farmer works, but he depends on rain, soil, sunlight, seed, time, and natural law. A doctor treats, but healing depends on the body’s inner intelligence. A person may earn, cook, study, build, and plan, but all these actions succeed only because Bhagavān’s order supports them.

Therefore, when we enjoy what has been given by the devatās, we must offer back. Offering back may be pūjā, prayer, namaskāra, dānam, feeding others, service, protecting nature, supporting dharma, or simply using resources with gratitude and reverence. Bhagavān does not need our food or possessions. Even a simple “thank you” through prayerful living is part of yajña.

The strong word in this shloka is stenaḥ — thief. A thief uses what belongs to another without permission, acknowledgment, or gratitude. The gifts of life are given through Bhagavān’s order. To enjoy them without reverence, gratitude, offering, or sharing is spiritual theft.

This shloka therefore teaches that life should not be divided into “sacred” and “ordinary.” Eating, working, driving, building, cooking, earning, studying, and resting can all become sacred when done with gratitude. A non-reverential life is mere karma. A reverential life is karma-yoga.
key_verses.story:
King Nṛga and the Brāhmaṇa’s Cow (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Nṛga was famous for generosity. He gave countless cows in charity to brāhmaṇas. These cows were not neglected animals. They were properly cared for, decorated, and given with respect. Nṛga’s intention was noble, and his reputation for dānam was great.
But one serious mistake occurred. A cow that had already been given to one brāhmaṇa wandered back into the royal herd. Not knowing this, King Nṛga later gave that same cow to another brāhmaṇa.
Soon the first brāhmaṇa saw the cow and said, “This cow was given to me.” The second brāhmaṇa replied, “The king has given her to me.” Both were speaking truth from their own side. The dispute reached King Nṛga.
Nṛga was deeply troubled. He did not dismiss the matter. He offered many cows in exchange, but the dispute could not be easily repaired. The issue was not the number of cows. The issue was that something belonging to one person had been given to another.
Because of this error involving another’s property, Nṛga later suffered the consequence and was born as a great lizard. Much later, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa released him.
This story illumines 3.12. Even a generous king could not treat what belonged to another as his own. The world and its blessings do not belong to the ego. They come through Bhagavān’s order. If we enjoy what is given by the devatās without acknowledging the giver, offering back, or using it responsibly, we fall into the attitude of theft.
Nṛga’s story teaches that receiving carries responsibility. Gratitude, care, and proper offering must accompany enjoyment.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka changes how we look at everything we use. Water from the tap is not “just water.” Food on the plate is not “just food.” Electricity, roads, phones, books, schools, temples, hospitals, medicines, clothes, and homes are not merely objects for consumption. They are gifts that come through nature, society, human effort, and Bhagavān’s laws.

When we forget this, we become careless. We waste food. We waste water. We pollute rivers. We exploit workers. We use public spaces badly. We consume without sharing. We enjoy benefits from parents, teachers, society, and culture without gratitude. This is the attitude the shloka calls theft.

In Indian life, this teaching is built into many simple practices. Before eating, we offer food to Bhagavān. Before starting a vehicle, people do namaskāra. Before building, we do bhūmi-pūjā. Before beginning work, we remember Gaṇeśa or Bhagavān. These are not empty customs. They remind us: “This is not mine alone. I receive through grace. Let me use it reverentially.”

This also applies to success. When we succeed, we may think, “I did it.” Effort matters, but effort alone is not everything. Health, opportunity, family support, teachers, social stability, intelligence, timing, and unseen grace all contribute. Remembering this gives humility in success.

The practical message is: enjoy, but do not enjoy like a thief. Receive with gratitude. Share what you can. Offer to Bhagavān. Use resources carefully. Say thank you through action. Then enjoyment itself becomes part of karma-yoga.
key_verses.reflection:
What blessings do I enjoy every day without consciously thanking Bhagavān or the people and forces that support me?
Where am I consuming more than I am contributing?
How can I offer back today — through prayer, sharing, careful use, service, or gratitude?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says, “When you get something, remember to say thank you and share.”
We get many gifts every day. We get food, water, air, sunlight, parents, teachers, friends, books, toys, and a home. We did not make all these by ourselves.
If someone gives us something and we grab it without saying thank you, that is not nice. If we keep taking and never give back, that is also not nice.
So before eating, we can thank Bhagavān. We can share snacks. We can avoid wasting water. We can take care of books and toys. We can help the people who help us.
Kṛṣṇa teaches that we should not enjoy like someone who only grabs. We should enjoy with a thankful heart.
kids_content.story:
Dev and the Borrowed Crayons
Dev loved coloring. One day in school, he forgot his crayon box. During art class, his friend Anika saw him sitting quietly and said, “You can use mine.”
Dev became happy. He used the red, blue, green, and yellow crayons. His picture looked beautiful. When the bell rang, he put the drawing in his bag and ran out to play.
The crayons were still on his desk.
Anika came back and saw two crayons on the floor and one broken. She felt sad. “Dev, I gave them to you. You did not even say thank you. You did not put them back.”
Dev felt ashamed. He had not meant to be rude. He was just excited about his picture.
The next day, Dev went to Anika and said, “I am sorry. Thank you for sharing your crayons.” He gave her a new yellow crayon to replace the broken one. After that, whenever he borrowed something, he returned it carefully and said thank you.
Dev learned that using something given by someone else comes with responsibility.
Kṛṣṇa teaches the same thing. The world gives us so much. We should not just take and enjoy. We should thank, share, and take care.
kids_content.reflection:
Who helped me today, and did I say thank you?
What is one thing I use every day that I should take better care of?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Desired enjoyments
Phrase: iṣṭān bhogān = desired enjoyments The devatās give the enjoyments and supports that human beings need and desire: food, water, health, shelter, clothing, prosperity, and the conditions for life. These are not separate from Bhagavān’s order.
Step 2: The devatās give
Phrase: hi vo devā dāsyante = the devatās will indeed give to you The devatās, presiding over natural forces, bless human beings through the functioning of nature and cosmic law. Rain, sunlight, fire, air, soil, and biological laws are expressions of this order.
Step 3: Nourished by yajña
Phrase: yajñabhāvitāḥ = nourished by yajña When human beings live a yajña-life — worship, gratitude, service, sharing, restraint, and harmony — the devatās are nourished. This means the cosmic and natural order is honored rather than violated.
Step 4: Gifts from them
Phrase: tair dattān = given by them The enjoyments we receive are gifts from the devatās. Human effort is included, but it is not the only factor. Many unseen conditions must cooperate for any result.
Step 5: Without offering back
Phrase: apradāya ebhyaḥ = without offering to them If one receives and enjoys without offering back, without gratitude, without worship, and without contribution, one breaks the yajña-cycle. Offering back may be ritual, prayer, dānam, service, environmental care, or reverential use.
Step 6: Enjoying selfishly
Phrase: yo bhuṅkte = whoever enjoys The issue is not enjoyment itself. Enjoyment becomes a problem when it is cut off from gratitude and responsibility. Enjoyment with reverence is allowed; enjoyment with greed and forgetfulness binds.
Step 7: Such a person is a thief
Phrase: stena eva saḥ = he is indeed a thief A thief uses what belongs to another without permission or acknowledgment. The gifts of life belong to Bhagavān’s order. To consume them without gratitude is spiritual theft.
Step 8: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka This verse transforms daily enjoyment into sacred responsibility. Receive what the devatās give, but do not merely consume. Offer, thank, share, and live reverentially. That is karma-yoga.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. The Golden Mongoose and the Poor Family’s Offering (Mahābhārata)
After Yudhiṣṭhira performed a grand sacrifice, many people praised its greatness. Kings, sages, guests, and citizens had been honored. Gifts had been given. Food had been distributed. Outwardly, it looked like a magnificent yajña.
Then a strange mongoose appeared. Half of its body was golden. It rolled on the sacrificial ground and then spoke in a surprising way. It said that this great sacrifice was not equal to a small act of offering it had once witnessed.
The mongoose told of a poor family during a terrible famine. After much hardship, the family had obtained a little flour. They prepared it and were about to eat. Their hunger was real. Their bodies needed that food.
Just then, a hungry guest arrived.
The father gave his share. The guest was still hungry. The mother gave her share. Still the guest was not satisfied. The son gave his share. Then the daughter-in-law also gave hers. One by one, each person gave up the food that could have sustained them.
They did not eat first and offer leftovers. They offered before enjoying.
The mongoose said that when it rolled on the ground where that flour had fallen, half of its body turned golden. Such was the greatness of that small but total offering.
This story fits 3.12 beautifully. The shloka warns against enjoying gifts without offering back. This poor family did the opposite. They received a little food and immediately turned it into yajña through hospitality, gratitude, and selflessness. The size of the offering was small; the spirit was vast.

2. The Fruit Seller Offers to Child Kṛṣṇa (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
In Vṛndāvana, a fruit seller walked through the village calling out with her basket of fruits. Children heard her voice, mothers came to the door, and the simple life of the village moved around her.
Little Kṛṣṇa heard the call and wanted fruit. Like a child, he ran inside and gathered a handful of grains to give in exchange. But his hands were tiny. As he hurried toward the fruit seller, most of the grains slipped through his fingers and fell on the ground.
By the time he reached her, only a few grains remained in his palms.
The fruit seller looked at him. She saw not a customer but the beautiful child of Vraja. Her heart melted. She did not calculate loss. She did not say, “This is not enough.” She filled Kṛṣṇa’s hands with fruits.
Later, when she looked into her basket, it was filled with jewels.
This story shows the beauty of offering. The fruit seller received only a little, but she gave with love. She did not hold tightly to what she had. What returned to her was far greater, but that was not her motive.
In the spirit of 3.12, life should not be only taking and enjoying. The right attitude is gratitude, offering, and willingness to give. When even ordinary exchange is touched by love and offering, it becomes sacred.

3. Draupadī’s Akṣaya Pātra and Kṛṣṇa’s Last Leaf (Mahābhārata)
During exile, the Pāṇḍavas lived in the forest. They had lost their kingdom, palace, and comforts. Yet Yudhiṣṭhira did not abandon dharma. Guests and sages still had to be honored.
Through divine grace, Draupadī had the Akṣaya Pātra, a vessel that could provide food each day until she herself had eaten. With that gift, the Pāṇḍavas fed brāhmaṇas, guests, and dependents. They did not treat the vessel as a private luxury. It was used for hospitality and dharma.
One day, after Draupadī had eaten and the vessel was empty, Sage Durvāsā arrived with many disciples. They went to bathe, expecting food afterward. Draupadī was distressed. The vessel would not produce again that day.
She prayed to Kṛṣṇa.
Kṛṣṇa came and asked for food. Draupadī was helpless, but Kṛṣṇa found one tiny leaf or particle remaining in the vessel. He ate it with satisfaction. Because Bhagavān was satisfied, Durvāsā and his disciples felt full and left without troubling the Pāṇḍavas.
This story shows how a divine gift should be used. The Akṣaya Pātra was not for selfish enjoyment. It served yajña, hospitality, and dharma. And when distress came, gratitude and surrender to Bhagavān protected them.
The connection to 3.12 is clear: what is given by the devatās or Bhagavān must not be consumed selfishly. It should be offered, shared, and used reverentially.

4. King Vena Stops Yajña and Society Suffers (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Vena came to power with arrogance. Instead of seeing kingship as a sacred responsibility, he saw himself as the center of everything. He forbade yajñas, offerings, and worship. He declared that people should honor him alone.
The sages were disturbed. Yajña was not merely a private ritual. It was the way society remained connected to Bhagavān, the devatās, nature, gratitude, and dharma. When Vena stopped yajña, he was cutting the link between receiving and offering.
The kingdom began to suffer. Without dharma, fear increased. Without yajña, reverence declined. Without reverence, people became selfish and disorderly. The ruler who should have protected the yajña-cycle had interrupted it.
The sages warned him, but he would not listen. His arrogance led to his downfall.
This story presents the opposite of 3.12. The shloka says that those who receive from the devatās must offer back. Vena wanted all honor and enjoyment for himself. He tried to consume the reverence owed to Bhagavān’s order. Such a life becomes destructive because it is rooted in ingratitude and spiritual theft.

5. Nahusha Falls from Indra’s Throne (Mahābhārata)
Nahusha once gained the throne of Indra when Indra was absent. To sit in that position was a great blessing. It was not an ordinary seat of power. It belonged to the order of the devatās and carried responsibility, humility, and dharma.
At first, Nahusha shone with the power of that position. But soon pride arose. Instead of receiving the gift with gratitude, he began to enjoy the status as though it belonged to him by personal greatness alone.
His arrogance grew so much that he ordered great sages to carry his palanquin. Among them was Agastya. Nahusha, intoxicated with power, became impatient and insulted the sages. In some tellings, he struck Agastya with his foot.
The fall was immediate. Cursed by the sage, Nahusha lost his heavenly position and fell from the world of the gods.
This story illustrates the warning of 3.12. When blessings are received from the devatās, they must be used with humility and reverence. If one enjoys divine gifts without gratitude, and worse, uses them for ego and disrespect, the enjoyment becomes theft-like. Nahusha did not offer back through dharma; he consumed the gift through pride, and he fell.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: इष्टान् भोगान् हि वः देवाः दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः । तैः दत्तान् अप्रदाय एभ्यः यः भुङ्क्ते स्तेनः एव सः ॥३.१२॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: इष्टान् | (iṣṭān) = desired भोगान् | (bhogān) = enjoyments, pleasures हि | (hi) = indeed वः | (vaḥ) = to you / for you देवाः | (devāḥ) = the devatās दास्यन्ते | (dāsyante) = will give यज्ञभाविताः | (yajñabhāvitāḥ) = nourished by yajña तैः | (taiḥ) = by them दत्तान् | (dattān) = given अप्रदाय | (apradāya) = without offering, without giving back एभ्यः | (ebhyaḥ) = to these, to them यः | (yaḥ) = who भुङ्क्ते | (bhuṅkte) = enjoys स्तेनः | (stenaḥ) = thief एव | (eva) = indeed सः | (saḥ) = he
rich_grammar.anvayah: यज्ञभाविताः देवाः वः इष्टान् भोगान् दास्यन्ते हि । तैः दत्तान् एभ्यः अप्रदाय यः भुङ्क्ते, सः स्तेनः एव ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: इष्टान् + भोगान् = इष्टान्भोगान् | भोगान् + हि = भोगान्हि | तैः + दत्तान् = तैर्दत्तान् | दत्तान् + अप्रदाय = दत्तानप्रदाय | अप्रदाय + एभ्यः = अप्रदायैभ्यः | स्तेनः + एव = स्तेन
rich_grammar.samasa: यज्ञभाविताः = यज्ञेन भाविताः Samāsa sense: तृतीया-तत्पुरुष-like expression; nourished or strengthened by yajña. | No major compound beyond this is central to the verse. Other words are best understood directly in the sentence.
rich_grammar.other: दास्यन्ते is future tense, आत्मनेपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम्: “they will give.” | भुङ्क्ते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम् from भुज्, meaning “enjoys” or “partakes.” | अप्रदाय is an indeclinable participial form meaning “without offering” or “without giving back.” It is central to the teaching. | वः is a second-person plural pronoun, here best understood as “to you” or “for you.” | इष्टान् and दत्तान् both qualify भोगान् in sense: the desired enjoyments, given by the devatās. | एभ्यः is चतुर्थी बहुवचनम्, “to these,” referring to the devatās. | स्तेनः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग, the predicate noun: “he is a thief.”

key_verses.final_takeaway: Enjoying the gifts of life without giving back is spiritual theft. True dharma means receiving with gratitude and contributing through one's own duties, ensuring the cycle of giving and receiving remains unbroken.

key_verses.title: Yajña Purifies, Selfishness Binds
key_verses.sanskrit:
यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः(स्) सन्तो(तः) मुच्यन्ते सर्वकिल्बिषैः ।
भुञ्जते ते त्वघं पापा(:) ये पचन्त्यात्मकारणात् ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
yajñaśiṣṭāśinaḥ(s) santo(aḥ) mucyante sarvakilbiṣaiḥ ।
bhuñjate te tvaghaṁ pāpā(ḥ) ye pacantyātmakāraṇāt ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Those who partake of what remains after offering to Bhagavān are freed from spiritual obstacles, while those who cook only for themselves consume sin.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the yajña teaching. In the previous shloka, he said that enjoying the gifts of the devatās without offering back is theft. Now he gives the positive and negative contrast in terms of food, because food is the most immediate example of receiving from Bhagavān’s order.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the yajña teaching. In the previous shloka, he said that enjoying the gifts of the devatās without offering back is theft. Now he gives the positive and negative contrast in terms of food, because food is the most immediate example of receiving from Bhagavān’s order.

Yajña-śiṣṭa means what remains after yajña, what remains after offering. In daily life, this is prasāda — food or anything received after first acknowledging Bhagavān. When we offer food to Bhagavān and then partake of it, we are not merely eating. We are receiving with gratitude. The food becomes connected with reverence, humility, and karma-yoga.

The important idea is not that Bhagavān physically needs our food. Bhagavān is not hungry. The offering is for our transformation. Before offering, the food is looked upon as “mine, for my enjoyment.” After offering, it becomes “Bhagavān’s gift, given back to me as prasāda.” This change of attitude is the essence.

Those who live in this reverential way are called santaḥ — noble people, refined people. They are freed from sarva-kilbiṣaiḥ, all impurities or obstacles. Here kilbiṣa means pāpa, and pāpa is not merely a moral label. Anything that obstructs spiritual growth is pāpa. Anything that strengthens moha, selfishness, greed, entitlement, and forgetfulness of Bhagavān becomes an obstacle.

On the other hand, those who cook only for themselves — ātma-kāraṇāt — eat agham, sin. This does not condemn cooking itself or eating itself. The problem is the attitude: “This is only for me. I take, I enjoy, I do not offer, I do not thank, I do not share.” Such a life strengthens ego and moha.

Therefore, 3.13 teaches us to convert consumption into worship. Food and every karma-phala can be received as īśvara-prasāda. A reverential life is karma-yoga. A non-reverential life remains mere karma and binds.
key_verses.story:
Vidura’s Simple Food Becomes Dear to Kṛṣṇa (Mahābhārata / Bhakti Tradition)
When Kṛṣṇa went to Hastināpura as a messenger of peace, Duryodhana arranged a royal welcome. Palaces were decorated. Fine dishes were prepared. Duryodhana wanted Kṛṣṇa to eat in his palace and accept his hospitality.
But Duryodhana’s heart was not humble. His invitation carried pride, politics, and calculation. He wanted honor from Kṛṣṇa, but he did not want to listen to dharma. Kṛṣṇa declined.
Instead, Kṛṣṇa went to Vidura’s home.
Vidura was not a king. His house was simple. He did not have Duryodhana’s wealth or royal arrangements. But his heart was full of devotion. When Kṛṣṇa arrived, Vidura was overwhelmed. The house became sacred, not because of golden vessels, but because Bhagavān himself had come and the host received him with love.
Vidura offered simple food with reverence. In later devotional tellings, even a humble offering — sometimes remembered as greens, sometimes as banana peels offered in a flood of devotion — became dear to Kṛṣṇa because it carried bhakti. Kṛṣṇa accepted not the luxury of the food, but the purity of the offering.
This story illumines 3.13. Food becomes spiritually meaningful when it is offered with reverence. What is eaten after offering becomes prasāda. The opposite of selfish consumption is not expensive ritual; it is a heart that first offers to Bhagavān and then receives with gratitude. Vidura’s simple food became yajña-śiṣṭa because it was given to Kṛṣṇa with devotion.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka makes daily eating spiritual. It teaches that food is not merely calories, taste, or fuel. Food comes through Bhagavān’s order: earth, water, sunlight, rain, farmers, animals, transport, family, cooks, health, and time. When we eat without gratitude, the mind becomes more entitled. When we eat as prasāda, the mind becomes refined.

In Indian homes, offering food before eating is not an empty custom. A small portion is offered to Bhagavān. We may say a prayer. We may mentally say, “This is yours.” Then we eat. The quantity of the offering need not be large. The important thing is reverence. Even medicine can be taken as prasāda because its healing capacity also belongs to Bhagavān’s order.

This teaching also applies beyond food. Salary can be received as prasāda. Success can be received as prasāda. A house, car, education, friendship, and health can all be received with gratitude. If we use them only for selfish enjoyment, they can strengthen bondage. If we offer, share, and use them dhārmically, they become part of karma-yoga.

The verse also warns against a self-centered kitchen and a self-centered life. If I cook only for myself, earn only for myself, learn only for myself, and enjoy only for myself, I am strengthening ego. But if I cook with sharing, earn with dānam, study with service, and enjoy with gratitude, life becomes yajña.

Receiving food and experiences as prasāda slowly weakens the delusion that the world itself is the source of my happiness or sorrow; it trains the mind to see that my attitude toward the world matters.

The practical message is simple: before enjoying, offer. After receiving, thank. While using, share. Then ordinary life becomes prasāda.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I usually eat with gratitude, or do I eat as though food is simply mine by right?
What result or experience in my life can I receive as īśvara-prasāda instead of immediately judging it as a source of happiness or sorrow?
Where in my life am I “cooking only for myself” — using resources without offering, thanking, or sharing?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says, “Before you enjoy something, remember Bhagavān and say thank you.”
When food comes to our plate, many things helped it come there. The sun helped. Rain helped. The farmer helped. Amma or Appa cooked. Bhagavān’s world helped.
So before eating, we can fold our hands and say, “Thank you, Bhagavān.” Then the food becomes prasāda. Prasāda means something we receive with a thankful heart.
If we only grab and eat without thanking or sharing, the heart becomes selfish. If we offer first and then eat, the heart becomes kind and clean.
kids_content.story:
Anika’s First Mango Slice
Anika loved mangoes. One summer afternoon, her grandfather cut a ripe mango into bright yellow slices. The smell filled the kitchen.
Anika quickly reached for the biggest slice.
Grandfather smiled and gently said, “One moment. Before we eat, let us offer first.”
Anika stopped. “But Bhagavān will not come and eat it.”
Grandfather placed one small slice on a little plate near the pūjā shelf. He folded his hands. “Bhagavān does not need the mango. We need to remember that the mango came because of Bhagavān’s world — the tree, rain, sun, soil, and many people.”
Anika also folded her hands. “Thank you, Bhagavān.”
After a moment, grandfather brought the slice back and said, “Now it is prasāda.”
Anika ate slowly. The mango tasted sweet, but her heart also felt happy. The next day, when mangoes were cut again, she herself said, “First we should offer.”
Anika learned that food becomes special when we remember Bhagavān before enjoying it.
kids_content.reflection:
Before I eat today, can I say thank you to Bhagavān?
What snack can I share instead of keeping it only for myself?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Those who eat yajña-śiṣṭa
Phrase: yajñaśiṣṭāśinaḥ = those who eat what remains after yajña This means those who partake of what has first been offered. In daily life, this is prasāda. The food is first connected to Bhagavān through offering, and then received with gratitude.
Step 2: The noble people
Phrase: santaḥ = noble people The santaḥ are those whose lives are refined by reverence. They do not consume casually or selfishly. They remember the giver behind the gift.
Step 3: Freedom from impurities
Phrase: mucyante sarvakilbiṣaiḥ = they are freed from all impurities Kilbiṣa means impurity or pāpa. In this teaching, pāpa means anything that obstructs spiritual growth. A prasāda-attitude weakens ego, greed, entitlement, and moha.
Step 4: The selfish enjoyers
Phrase: bhuñjate te tu agham pāpāḥ = but those sinful people eat sin Those who enjoy without offering and gratitude strengthen pāpa. They may be eating good food physically, but spiritually they are feeding ego and forgetfulness.
Step 5: Cooking only for oneself
Phrase: ye pacanti ātmakāraṇāt = those who cook for their own sake alone Cooking is not condemned. Eating is not condemned. The problem is ātmakāraṇāt — only for oneself, with no offering, no sharing, no reverence, and no gratitude.
Step 6: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse teaches that the same food can either purify or bind. If received as yajña-śiṣṭa, it becomes prasāda and purifies. If consumed selfishly, it becomes a source of pāpa. The difference is the attitude.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Śabarī Offers Berries to Rāma (Rāmāyaṇa)
Śabarī lived in the forest āśrama of her guru. Her life was simple. She cleaned the āśrama, kept the path ready, and waited for the arrival of Rāma, as her guru had promised.
Years passed. Śabarī grew old, but her devotion did not become weak. Every day she gathered fruits from the forest, hoping that Rāma might come that day.
At last, Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa arrived. Śabarī’s heart overflowed. She had no royal dishes, no grand feast, and no expensive vessels. She had only forest fruits and devotion.
She carefully tasted the berries to make sure only the sweet ones were offered to Rāma. Outwardly, this was not formal ritual correctness. But Rāma saw her heart. He accepted the berries with joy because they were offered with love.
This story connects directly to 3.13. The value of food is transformed by offering. Śabarī’s berries became sacred because they were not kept for herself; they were offered to Bhagavān. Food given with devotion and then received as prasāda purifies the heart.

2. Sudāmā Offers Flattened Rice to Kṛṣṇa (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Sudāmā lived in poverty with his wife and children. One day his wife gently urged him to visit Kṛṣṇa, his childhood friend. The family had almost nothing, but she gathered a small handful of flattened rice and tied it in a cloth for Sudāmā to carry as a gift.
Sudāmā reached Dvārakā shy and hesitant. Kṛṣṇa saw him and ran to embrace him. He seated Sudāmā with honor, washed his feet, and treated him with deep love.
Sudāmā tried to hide the poor gift. How could he offer such a small thing in a palace? But Kṛṣṇa lovingly took the bundle from him. He ate the flattened rice with delight, as though it were the finest food.
Sudāmā did not ask for wealth. He returned home quietly. When he reached, his hut had become a beautiful dwelling by Kṛṣṇa’s grace. Yet his heart remained simple and devoted.
This story illumines 3.13. A small food offering, when given to Bhagavān with humility, becomes sacred. The point is not the richness of the item, but the reverence of the heart. Food kept only for oneself binds; food offered to Bhagavān becomes prasāda and purifies.

3. The Gopīs Offer Food to Kṛṣṇa and His Friends (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
One day, Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma were in the forest with the cowherd boys. The boys became hungry and asked Kṛṣṇa for food. Nearby, some brāhmaṇas were performing a sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa sent the boys to ask them for food.
The boys went respectfully and requested food in Kṛṣṇa’s name. But the brāhmaṇas were absorbed in the outer ritual. They heard the request, but they did not respond. The sacrifice was going on, yet the living presence of Bhagavān was being missed.
Kṛṣṇa then sent the boys to the wives of those brāhmaṇas. When the women heard Kṛṣṇa’s name, their hearts overflowed with devotion. They gathered food immediately and ran to offer it to Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma.
The food became blessed because it was offered with love. The women did not keep it only for themselves or merely for ritual formality. They gave it to Bhagavān himself, and Kṛṣṇa accepted their devotion.
This story connects directly to 3.13. Food becomes sacred when it is offered to Bhagavān. Outer cooking or ritual alone is not enough; the heart must offer. The wives of the brāhmaṇas turned food into yajña-śiṣṭa through devotion, while the ritualists temporarily missed the living meaning of yajña.

4. The Poor Family Offers Their Last Flour (Mahābhārata)
During a severe famine, a poor brāhmaṇa family somehow obtained a small measure of flour. They prepared it with care. For them, this was not a small snack; it was the food that would keep them alive.
Just as they were about to eat, a hungry guest came to the door. The family faced a real test. Their bodies were weak. Hunger pressed them. Yet hospitality was dharma.
The father gave his portion to the guest. The guest was still hungry. The mother gave hers. Still the guest was not satisfied. The son gave his share. Finally, the daughter-in-law gave hers. Each one offered before eating.
The family remained hungry, but their act became spiritually radiant. Later, the golden mongoose declared that this small offering was greater than a grand royal sacrifice, because it was total and selfless.
This story reflects 3.13 with great force. The family did not cook only for themselves. They transformed their food into yajña by offering it to a guest before enjoying it. Those who eat yajña-śiṣṭa are purified; those who live only for themselves eat pāpa. This family chose offering over selfish consumption.

5. Rantideva Gives Away His Last Water (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Rantideva once went many days without proper food and water. At last, a little food and water came to him and his family. Their hunger and thirst were real.
As he was about to eat, a guest arrived. Rantideva gave food. Another came, and he gave again. Then another came, and he gave again. Each time, what remained became less.
Finally, only a little water was left. Rantideva was extremely thirsty. Then a suffering person came and asked for water. Rantideva gave even that last water, praying that the suffering of others may be relieved.
The scene is powerful because he did not offer leftovers after satisfying himself. He offered while still in need. His food and water became instruments of compassion and worship.
This story connects to 3.13 because it shows the opposite of cooking only for oneself. Rantideva did not treat food and water as private possessions meant only for his body. He turned them into yajña through offering. Such food purifies because it is touched by selflessness and reverence.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः सन्तः मुच्यन्ते सर्वकिल्बिषैः । भुञ्जते ते तु अघम् पापाः ये पचन्ति आत्मकारणात् ॥३.१३॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः | (yajñaśiṣṭāśinaḥ) = those who eat what remains after yajña सन्तः | (santaḥ) = noble people, refined people मुच्यन्ते | (mucyante) = are freed सर्वकिल्बिषैः | (sarvakilbiṣaiḥ) = from all impurities, from all pāpas भुञ्जते | (bhuñjate) = they eat, they enjoy ते | (te) = they तु | (tu) = but, indeed अघम् | (agham) = sin, pāpa पापाः | (pāpāḥ) = sinful people ये | (ye) = who पचन्ति | (pacanti) = cook आत्मकारणात् | (ātmakāraṇāt) = for their own sake
rich_grammar.anvayah: यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः सन्तः सर्वकिल्बिषैः मुच्यन्ते । ये आत्मकारणात् पचन्ति, ते पापाः तु अघं भुञ्जते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: यज्ञशिष्ट + अशिनः = यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः | सन्तः + मुच्यन्ते = सन्तो | ते + तु = ते | तु + अघम् = त्वघम् | पचन्ति + आत्मकारणात् = पचन्त्यात्मकारणात्
rich_grammar.samasa: यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः = यज्ञशिष्टम् अश्नन्ति इति Samāsa sense: उपपद-तत्पुरुष / बहुव्रीहि-like functional expression; those who eat what remains after yajña. | सर्वकिल्बिषैः = सर्वैः किल्बिषैः Samāsa name: कर्मधारय / adjective-noun compound sense; all impurities or all pāpas. | आत्मकारणात् = आत्मनः कारणात् Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; for the sake of oneself.
rich_grammar.other: मुच्यन्ते is passive: “are freed.” The noble people are freed from impurities. | भुञ्जते is आत्मनेपदम् from भुज्, meaning “eat” or “enjoy.” Here it has a strong moral sense: they consume sin. | पचन्ति is लट्-लकारः, परस्मैपदम्, meaning “they cook.” | सन्तः and पापाः are both प्रथमा बहुवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग and describe two contrasting groups. | अघम् is द्वितीया एकवचनम्, the object of भुञ्जते: “they eat sin.” | सर्वकिल्बिषैः is used with मुच्यन्ते to indicate freedom from all impurities. It is grammatically instrumental plural, with an ablative-like sense in English: “from all impurities.” | तु marks contrast between the noble prasāda-eaters and the selfish consumers.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The spirit in which we consume matters deeply. Eating and living after first offering to Bhagavān purifies the heart and frees one from inner obstacles. Selfishness in action and consumption becomes a source of binding.

key_verses.title: The Cosmic Cycle of Sustenance
key_verses.sanskrit:
अन्नाद्भवन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः ।
यज्ञाद्भवति पर्जन्यो(यः) यज्ञः(ख्) कर्मसमुद्भवः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
annādbhavanti bhūtāni parjanyādannasambhavaḥ ।
yajñādbhavati parjanyo(aḥ) yajñaḥ(kh) karmasamudbhavaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Beings are born from food; food comes from rain; rain comes from yajña; and yajña arises from karma.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now explains the yajña-cycle more fully. The previous verses taught that human beings must live a life of yajña, gratitude, offering, and mutual nourishment. Now the verse shows why this is not merely a religious sentiment. It is the structure of life itself.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now explains the yajña-cycle more fully. The previous verses taught that human beings must live a life of yajña, gratitude, offering, and mutual nourishment. Now the verse shows why this is not merely a religious sentiment. It is the structure of life itself.

All beings depend on food. The body is sustained by food. Plants, animals, humans, and all embodied beings need nourishment. Food does not appear independently. Food depends on rain. Rain nourishes the soil, fills rivers, supports crops, and sustains life.

Rain depends on yajña. Here yajña is not merely a ritual fire offering. It is the dhārmic way of life by which human beings live in harmony with the cosmic and natural order. When human beings live with reverence, contribution, restraint, and responsibility, the order is preserved. When they live with greed and exploitation, the order is disturbed.

Yajña depends on karma. This means yajña is not merely an attitude in the mind. It must express as action: offering, sharing, service, worship, dānam, care for nature, protection of animals, study of scripture, and duties performed properly. Karma-yoga is therefore dharma — that which maintains the harmony of creation.

This verse gives a complete chain: karma → yajña → rain → food → beings. If karma is selfish and adhārmic, yajña is weakened. If yajña is weakened, harmony with nature is disturbed. If nature is disturbed, food and life suffer. But if karma becomes karma-yoga, yajña is maintained, rain and food are supported, and beings flourish.

The teaching is especially important because human beings have free will. Other living beings are largely programmed to live within nature’s balance. Human beings alone can either preserve the world or damage it. Therefore, a karma-yogī does not live as an isolated consumer. He lives as a responsible participant in Bhagavān’s order.
key_verses.story:
Gautama Ṛṣi’s Āśrama Nourishes Sages During Drought (Purāṇic Tradition)
A severe drought once struck the land. The sky gave no rain. Rivers shrank. Fields dried. Plants withered. Animals and people suffered. Even sages living in forests found it difficult to continue their daily life and worship.
In that difficult time, Gautama Ṛṣi performed tapas and lived with dharma. By the grace of Varuṇa, water became available in his āśrama. Crops could grow there. Food was cooked. The āśrama, instead of becoming a place of fear, became a place of nourishment.
Many sages came to Gautama’s āśrama for shelter. He did not say, “This water and food are only for me.” He welcomed them. He fed them. The āśrama became a living yajña, where what was received from the devatās was shared with others.
The tension was real: drought had broken the normal flow of life. The action was dhārmic: Gautama preserved tapas, reverence, food, water, and hospitality. The result was that beings were sustained.
This story illustrates 3.14. Food sustains beings; food depends on rain and water; rain and water belong to the devatā-order; and human beings must respond through yajña and karma. Gautama’s āśrama shows the cycle working properly: divine blessing was received, food was produced, and beings were nourished through dhārmic action.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka teaches that life is interconnected. Food is not just something bought from a store. Food depends on soil, rain, rivers, farmers, seeds, sunlight, insects, animals, transport, cooking, and social cooperation. If any part of the chain is damaged, life is affected.

A modern person may think, “I earn money, so food is mine.” But money alone cannot produce food. If rain fails, if soil is poisoned, if rivers dry, if farmers suffer, if seeds are damaged, if climate becomes unstable, money cannot replace the order of life. This verse trains us to see the whole chain.

In Indian life, this vision is already present in many practices. We offer food before eating. We revere rivers. We worship cows. We do bhūmi-pūjā before using land. We celebrate harvest festivals. We feed guests and animals. These are not empty customs. They remind us that life is received through a sacred cycle and must be protected through yajña.

The verse also expands our idea of karma-yoga. Karma-yoga is not only personal mental purity. It is dharma — a way of acting that maintains harmony. Honest work, careful use of water, avoiding food waste, supporting farmers, feeding others, protecting trees, caring for animals, studying and teaching dharma, and doing one’s duties responsibly are all part of the yajña-cycle.

The practical message is: when we act, we are not acting in isolation. Every action either supports the cycle or disturbs it. A karma-yogī asks: “Is my action helping the harmony of life, or am I taking from the chain without giving back?”
key_verses.reflection:
When I eat, do I remember the whole chain — rain, soil, farmers, animals, society, and Bhagavān’s order?
Which daily action of mine supports the yajña-cycle, and which action may be disturbing it?
How can I make one ordinary karma today into karma-yoga that preserves harmony?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says, “Food helps living beings live.”
Where does food come from? Food comes from plants. Plants need rain, sunlight, soil, and care. Rain and nature are gifts from Bhagavān’s world. People also have to do their part: farmers grow food, families cook, and we should not waste.
So when we eat, we should remember many helpers. The sun helped. Rain helped. The farmer helped. The cook helped. Bhagavān helped.
We should also help the cycle. We can avoid wasting food. We can water plants. We can keep water clean. We can thank Bhagavān before eating. We can share food.
That is yajña: receiving with thanks and giving back with care.
kids_content.story:
Nila and the Rice Grain
Nila was eating lunch after school. On her plate was a small mound of rice, dal, and vegetables. She was hungry, so she ate quickly. But near the end, she pushed some rice to the side.
“I am full,” she said. “I do not want this.”
Her grandmother looked at the rice and said gently, “Do you know how many friends helped this rice come to your plate?”
Nila laughed. “Friends? It is just rice.”
Grandmother picked up one grain and said, “This grain had many helpers. The farmer planted it. The soil held it. Rain watered it. The sun warmed it. Someone harvested it. Someone brought it to the shop. Amma cooked it. Bhagavān’s world helped all along.”
Nila looked at the grain again. It did not look ordinary anymore.
Grandmother said, “Take only what you can eat. If extra remains, we should not waste it. Food is precious.”
The next day, Nila served herself a little less first. When she was still hungry, she took more. She also helped water the tulasī plant near the kitchen.
Nila learned that food is part of a big chain. Kṛṣṇa teaches the same thing: beings live because of food, food comes from rain, and we must care for the cycle with gratitude.
kids_content.reflection:
Before I eat today, can I remember one helper who helped my food come to me?
Can I take only the food I need and avoid wasting it?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Beings arise from food
Phrase: annāt bhavanti bhūtāni = beings arise from food All embodied beings depend on food. Food nourishes the body, supports life, and enables action, worship, study, and spiritual pursuit.
Step 2: Food comes from rain
Phrase: parjanyāt annasambhavaḥ = food is born from rain Food production depends on rain and water. Rain sustains soil, crops, rivers, plants, animals, and human life.
Step 3: Rain comes from yajña
Phrase: yajñāt bhavati parjanyaḥ = rain comes from yajña Yajña here means a dhārmic, reverential way of life that maintains harmony with Bhagavān’s natural order. It includes worship, offering, contribution, restraint, and responsible living.
Step 4: Yajña comes from karma
Phrase: yajñaḥ karmasamudbhavaḥ = yajña is born of karma Yajña must be expressed through action. Good intentions alone are not enough. Duty, sharing, dānam, worship, service, protection of nature, and responsible work are the karmas that sustain yajña.
Step 5: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse presents karma-yoga as dharma. Dharma is that which sustains harmony. Human action must support the yajña-cycle so that nature, food, beings, and spiritual life can flourish.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Vasiṣṭha’s Nandinī Feeds Viśvāmitra’s Army (Rāmāyaṇa / Purāṇic Tradition)
King Viśvāmitra once came with his army to the āśrama of Sage Vasiṣṭha. A royal army was not easy to feed. Soldiers, attendants, animals, and the king himself all needed food and care.
Viśvāmitra may have expected the simple āśrama to be unable to host them. But Vasiṣṭha welcomed them with calm hospitality. He called upon Nandinī, the divine cow. Through her power, abundant food appeared. The entire army was fed.
Viśvāmitra was astonished. He saw that the wealth of the āśrama was not ordinary possession. It came from tapas, dharma, and sacred order. Wanting that power for himself, he tried to take Nandinī by force. But Nandinī belonged to the dhārmic life of the sage, not to royal greed.
The tension revealed an important truth. Food and abundance are not merely products of power. They arise from harmony with dharma. Vasiṣṭha’s āśrama nourished many because it lived in yajña-bhāva; Viśvāmitra’s desire to seize showed the opposite attitude.
This story connects to 3.14 because beings are sustained by food, and food ultimately depends on harmony with the sacred order. Vasiṣṭha’s hospitality shows yajña expressed as action: what is received is shared, and through dharma, many beings are nourished.

2. Manu Preserves the Seeds of Life Through the Flood (Matsya Purāṇa)
King Manu once found a tiny fish seeking protection. The fish asked Manu to save it from larger creatures. Manu placed it in a small vessel. But the fish grew. Manu moved it to a larger vessel, then to a pond, then to a river, and finally to the ocean.
The fish revealed itself as Bhagavān in the form of Matsya and warned Manu of a great flood. Manu was instructed to gather the seeds of life, the sages, and what would be needed to continue creation. When the flood came, Matsya guided the boat safely through the waters.
The world was covered by water, but the seeds were preserved. When the waters receded, life could begin again. Food, plants, beings, and dharma could continue.
This story has a powerful connection to 3.14. Beings depend on food, and food depends on the preservation of seed, water, and order. Manu’s action was not selfish survival; it was dhārmic responsibility for the continuity of life. Karma aligned with Bhagavān’s guidance preserved the cycle of beings and food.

3. Parīkṣit Protects Dharma and the Earth-Cow (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Parīkṣit once saw a disturbing scene. Dharma, appearing as a bull, was standing on one leg, weakened. The Earth, appearing as a cow, was grieving. A cruel figure was attacking them. The king immediately understood that this was not merely violence against animals; it was an attack on the order that sustains life.
Parīkṣit took up his royal duty. He questioned the situation, protected Dharma and Earth, and confronted the force of adharma. A king’s responsibility is not only to punish crime but to protect the conditions under which life can flourish.
The Earth-cow represents nourishment. Dharma-bull represents the moral order. If dharma is weakened, earth’s nourishment is also threatened. If the earth is harmed, beings suffer.
Parīkṣit’s action restored protection. He did not remain indifferent. He used his role to defend the harmony between dharma and the earth.
This story connects to 3.14 because food, beings, rain, yajña, and karma all depend on harmony. When dharma is protected through proper action, the earth can nourish beings. When adharma attacks the earth, the yajña-cycle is endangered.

4. King Janaka Ploughs the Sacred Field and Receives Sītā (Rāmāyaṇa Tradition)
King Janaka was not only a ruler seated in a palace. He was also a guardian of the land and the people. He understood that the prosperity of a kingdom depended on earth, rain, agriculture, dharma, and Bhagavān’s grace.
During a sacred agricultural rite, Janaka himself took the plough and entered the field. The scene was full of reverence. The king’s hands held the plough. The earth was approached not as property to exploit, but as a sacred mother who nourishes all beings.
As Janaka ploughed, the blade touched something hidden in the earth. The ground opened, and a divine child was discovered in the furrow. Janaka was filled with wonder. He received the child as a blessing from the earth and named her Sītā, because she was found in the sītā, the furrow.
This was not ordinary agriculture alone. It was action joined with sacredness. The field, the plough, the king’s effort, the earth’s blessing, and divine grace all came together.
This story connects to 3.14 because food and life depend on the sacred relationship between human action, earth, rain, and yajña. Janaka’s ploughing shows karma performed with reverence, and the earth’s gift of Sītā shows that when human effort honors the order, the earth becomes a source of blessing.

5. Jamadagni’s Kāmadhenu Feeds the Royal Guests (Mahābhārata / Purāṇic Tradition)
Sage Jamadagni lived in an āśrama with his wife Reṇukā and the divine cow Kāmadhenu. The āśrama was simple, but it was rich in tapas, dharma, and sacred power.
One day, King Kārtavīrya Arjuna came with his retinue. A king traveling with attendants and soldiers required great hospitality. In an ordinary hut, such a visit could become a burden. But Jamadagni received the guests according to dharma.
Through Kāmadhenu’s power, abundant food and hospitality were provided for everyone. The king saw that the sage’s āśrama possessed a power far beyond ordinary wealth. Instead of honoring the dhārmic source of that abundance, greed arose in his mind. He wanted Kāmadhenu for himself.
The tension turned sharp. What was meant for yajña, hospitality, and dharma was now desired as an object of possession and power. In the larger traditional account, this greed leads to grave consequences and conflict.
This story connects to 3.14 because food and abundance arise properly when action is aligned with yajña and dharma. Jamadagni uses Kāmadhenu to nourish guests; the king’s greed wants to seize the source of nourishment. The verse teaches that beings depend on food, food depends on rain and sacred order, and yajña depends on karma. When karma is dhārmic, abundance nourishes; when karma becomes greedy, the cycle is disturbed.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: अन्नात् भवन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यात् अन्नसम्भवः । यज्ञात् भवति पर्जन्यः यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भवः ॥३.१४॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: अन्नात् | (annāt) = from food भवन्ति | (bhavanti) = arise, are born, become भूतानि | (bhūtāni) = beings, living beings पर्जन्यात् | (parjanyāt) = from rain अन्नसम्भवः | (annasambhavaḥ) = the origin/production of food यज्ञात् | (yajñāt) = from yajña भवति | (bhavati) = arises, comes पर्जन्यः | (parjanyaḥ) = rain यज्ञः | (yajñaḥ) = yajña, sacred offering/action कर्मसमुद्भवः | (karmasamudbhavaḥ) = born of karma, arising from action
rich_grammar.anvayah: अन्नात् भूतानि भवन्ति । पर्जन्यात् अन्नसम्भवः भवति । यज्ञात् पर्जन्यः भवति । यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भवः भवति ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: अन्नात् + भवन्ति = अन्नाद्भवन्ति | पर्जन्यात् + अन्नसम्भवः = पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः | यज्ञात् + भवति = यज्ञाद्भवति
rich_grammar.samasa: अन्नसम्भवः = अन्नस्य सम्भवः Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; the origin or production of food. | कर्मसमुद्भवः = कर्मणः समुद्भवः Samāsa name: षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः; born of karma, arising from action.
rich_grammar.other: अन्नात्, पर्जन्यात्, and यज्ञात् are पञ्चमी विभक्ति forms showing source or cause: “from food,” “from rain,” and “from yajña.” | भूतानि is प्रथमा बहुवचनम् नपुंसकलिङ्ग and is the subject of भवन्ति. | अन्नसम्भवः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग. The verb भवति is understood from the next phrase or supplied in anvaya: “the origin of food is from rain.” | पर्जन्यः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग and is the subject of भवति. | यज्ञः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग and is the subject in the final clause. | कर्मसमुद्भवः is प्रथमा एकवचनम् पुंलिङ्ग, agreeing with यज्ञः.

key_verses.final_takeaway: All life is sustained through an interconnected cycle: beings depend on food, food on rain, rain on yajña, and yajña on karma. Understanding this web of mutual sustenance reveals why selfless action is essential.

meaning.caption: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa teaching Arjuna in the chariot, showing a glowing cycle: karma leading to yajña, yajña bringing rain, rain producing food, and food nourishing beings

modern_life.caption: A modern Indian family tracing one grain of rice back to rain, soil, farmer, sunlight, river, cow, cook, and Bhagavān’s order, all shown as a warm golden chain

kids_story.caption: Nila sitting at lunch with rice left on her plate, grandmother gently holding one grain and explaining all the helpers behind it

kids_explain.caption: Nila carefully taking only the food she needs and then watering a tulasī plant near the kitchen, learning gratitude for the food cycle

detailed_meaning.caption: A symbolic wheel of life with four sections labeled karma, yajña, rain, and food, with plants, animals, humans, rivers, clouds, and sunlight connected in harmony

key_verses.title: The Veda, Yajña, and the Infinite
key_verses.sanskrit:
कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भवं(वँ) विद्धि ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भवम् ।
तस्मात्सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं(यँ) यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम् ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
karma brahmodbhavaṁ(v̐) viddhi brahmākṣarasamudbhavam ।
tasmātsarvagataṁ brahma nityaṁ(y̐) yajñe pratiṣṭhitam ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Know that karma is born of the Veda, the Veda is born of Bhagavān, and therefore the all-pervading Veda is ever established in yajña.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the yajña-cycle. In the previous shloka, he showed the chain: beings depend on food, food comes from rain, rain comes from yajña, and yajña comes from karma. Now he explains where this karma-yoga way of life is known from.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the yajña-cycle. In the previous shloka, he showed the chain: beings depend on food, food comes from rain, rain comes from yajña, and yajña comes from karma. Now he explains where this karma-yoga way of life is known from.

Karma brahmodbhavam means karma is born from brahma, and here brahma means the Veda. The harmonious life of karma-yoga — a life of sacrifice, sharing, dānam, yajña, and responsibility — is taught by the Veda. Human beings may not discover this complete dhārmic order merely through personal preference or changing social theories. The Veda reveals how to live in harmony with the whole.

Then Bhagavān says brahmākṣarasamudbhavam — the Veda itself is born from akṣaram, the Imperishable Bhagavān. This is important because the Veda is not treated as a human invention. Human intellect has limits. Human ideas change from time to time. But the Vedic teaching has the authority of Bhagavān’s vision of creation. The creator alone fully knows how creation is to be handled.

Therefore, Bhagavān says tasmāt sarvagataṁ brahma — therefore the Veda is all-pervading. In a Vedic society, Vedic teaching enters every part of life: eating, study, family, work, dānam, rituals, hospitality, ecology, duties, values, and spiritual pursuit. It is not confined to a temple or a book.

Finally, nityaṁ yajñe pratiṣṭhitam means the Veda is always established in yajña. Wherever there is genuine sacrifice, sharing, offering, service, and dānam, there the Vedic teaching is alive. If the Veda is only recited but not lived through yajña, its spirit is missed. If a person lives a life of contribution and offering, that person is participating in the Vedic vision.

So the teaching of 3.15 is: karma-yoga is not a personal opinion, social fashion, or optional moral preference. It is the Vedic way of maintaining harmony, and the Veda itself comes from Bhagavān. Therefore a life of yajña is sacred, authoritative, and necessary.
key_verses.story:
Brahmā Receives the Veda from Bhagavān (Purāṇic / Vedic Tradition)
At the beginning of creation, Brahmā appeared on the lotus arising from Bhagavān Viṣṇu. Around him was vastness. The worlds had to be created, beings had to be guided, and dharma had to be established. But Brahmā did not begin creation as an independent inventor.
He performed tapas and turned toward Bhagavān. Through that divine grace, the Vedic wisdom was revealed to him. The Veda became the guide by which creation could be ordered, duties could be known, yajñas could be performed, and human life could be directed toward both prosperity and mokṣa.
The tension in the story is important. Brahmā had the responsibility of creation, but responsibility alone was not enough. He needed knowledge. Without divine instruction, creation would be directionless. With the Veda, creation received order.
Brahmā then became the first teacher of Vedic knowledge in the created world. The Veda did not arise as a human theory. It came from Bhagavān and entered creation as the manual for dharma, yajña, and spiritual life.
This story connects directly to 3.15. The verse says karma is born of the Veda, and the Veda is born of akṣaram, Bhagavān. Brahmā receiving the Veda shows that the dhārmic life of yajña is not invented by human preference; it is revealed by Bhagavān for the harmony of creation.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka teaches us not to treat dharma as a casual opinion. Many people say, “I will decide for myself what is right.” Personal thinking is useful, but it is limited. Human beings are influenced by likes, dislikes, convenience, social pressure, and changing trends. What feels right today may be rejected tomorrow.

The Veda gives a larger vision. It teaches a way of life based on harmony, sacrifice, sharing, restraint, gratitude, and spiritual growth. These values are not for Bhagavān’s benefit. They are for our own well-being, just as a manual is for the user’s benefit.

In Indian life, this means that practices like offering food, doing dānam, serving guests, honoring parents and teachers, studying scripture, protecting nature, observing vrata, doing pūjā, and sharing knowledge are not random customs. They are expressions of a Vedic vision of yajña. Some details may vary by family or region, but the underlying principle is contribution and reverence.

This shloka also asks us to respect the source of dharma. We need not blindly imitate every practice without understanding. But we should not dismiss the śāstra merely because modern taste does not immediately understand it. If something appears unclear, the mature response is to study it properly with a sampradāya teacher.

The practical message is: let life be guided by śāstra, not only by mood. Let action become yajña. Let earning include giving. Let learning include sharing. Let receiving include gratitude. Wherever sacrifice and sharing are practiced, the Vedic teaching is alive.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I allowing śāstra to guide my idea of right action, or am I relying only on personal convenience?
Where in my life is the Vedic spirit of yajña alive — in giving, sharing, serving, studying, or offering?
When I do not understand a traditional practice, do I dismiss it quickly, or do I try to understand its dhārmic value?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says, “There is a wise guide for how to live well.”
When we buy a toy, game, or machine, we may get instructions. The instructions help us use it properly. If we ignore them, the toy may break or the game may not work.
In the same way, Bhagavān has given wise teachings called the Veda. The Veda teaches us to live with kindness, sharing, prayer, respect, and helpful actions.
When we share food, help others, say thank you, pray, and do our duties, we are living the way the Veda teaches. That is yajña.
So Kṛṣṇa is telling Arjuna: “Do not act only by your mood. Follow the wise teaching and make your actions sacred.”
kids_content.story:
Rohan and the Model Airplane
Rohan received a model airplane kit for his birthday. The box had many pieces: wings, wheels, stickers, tiny screws, and a small instruction book.
Rohan was excited. He said, “I do not need the instructions. I know how to build it.”
He pushed pieces together quickly. One wing tilted. The wheels would not stay. A small screw was left over. When he tried to roll the airplane, it fell to one side.
His sister Meera picked up the instruction book and said, “Let us try step by step.”
Rohan sighed, but he agreed. They opened the instructions. First came the body. Then the wings. Then the wheels. Then the stickers. Slowly the airplane began to look right.
When they finished, it rolled smoothly across the floor. Rohan smiled. “The instructions knew better than I did.”
Meera said, “That is why they came with the box.”
Kṛṣṇa teaches that life also has wise instructions. The Veda teaches us how to live with sharing, prayer, kindness, and duty. When we follow that guidance, our actions become yajña and life works better.
kids_content.reflection:
When someone gives me good instructions, do I listen carefully?
What is one good rule from home or prayer that helps me live kindly?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Know the source of karma
Phrase: karma brahmodbhavaṁ viddhi = know karma to be born of brahma Here brahma means the Veda. Karma, especially karma-yoga and yajña-based action, is known from the Veda. The Veda teaches the duties and values that sustain harmony.
Step 2: The Veda comes from Bhagavān
Phrase: brahma akṣarasamudbhavam = the Veda is born of akṣaram Akṣaram means the Imperishable Bhagavān. The Veda is not a human invention; it comes from Bhagavān. Therefore, the Vedic teaching has a sacred authority beyond changing human opinion.
Step 3: Therefore the Veda is all-pervading
Phrase: tasmāt sarvagataṁ brahma = therefore the Veda is all-pervading The Veda pervades life by guiding all aspects of dharma: worship, family, society, learning, dānam, yajña, food, values, duties, and mokṣa.
Step 4: The Veda is established in yajña
Phrase: nityaṁ yajñe pratiṣṭhitam = always established in yajña The Vedic teaching is especially alive wherever yajña is practiced. Yajña here includes sacrifice, sharing, dānam, worship, and contribution. When people live yajña, the Veda is not merely spoken; it is lived.
Step 5: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka This verse completes the source-chain behind the yajña-cycle. Karma-yoga comes from the Veda; the Veda comes from Bhagavān; therefore, a yajña-life is not optional personal preference but the revealed way to maintain harmony and spiritual growth.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Vyāsa Arranges the Veda for the World (Mahābhārata / Purāṇic Tradition)
As time passed, human memory and discipline declined. The Vedic revelation was vast, and not everyone could preserve it properly. If the knowledge was not arranged and transmitted carefully, people would lose touch with dharma, yajña, and the way of life that sustains harmony.
Bhagavān Vyāsa saw this need. He did not treat the Veda as a private possession. He arranged the one Veda into four: Ṛg, Yajur, Sāma, and Atharva. He entrusted them to disciples who could preserve and teach them.
This was not merely literary editing. It was an act of compassion for society. By arranging the Veda, Vyāsa helped people know the karmas, yajñas, duties, values, and spiritual teachings appropriate for human life.
The resolution is that Vedic wisdom became more accessible for preservation and transmission. The stream of dharma could continue through teachers, students, recitation, study, and practice.
This story connects to 3.15 because the verse says karma is born of the Veda. Without Vedic teaching being preserved, people would not clearly know the yajña-based way of life. Vyāsa’s work shows how the Veda guides karma and keeps yajña alive in society.

2. Naciketas Learns the Fire Sacrifice from Yama (Kaṭha Upaniṣad)
Naciketas went to the abode of Yama and waited there for three nights without food or welcome. When Yama returned, he felt responsible for the lapse in hospitality and granted Naciketas three boons.
For the second boon, Naciketas asked to know the fire sacrifice that leads to heavenly good. Yama taught him the details of that yajña — how it is to be known, arranged, and performed. Naciketas listened carefully and repeated the teaching back correctly.
Yama was pleased. He said that this fire would be known by Naciketas’s name. The teaching was not casual. It had to be learned from a competent teacher, retained properly, and practiced with precision.
The tension here is between guessing and receiving proper teaching. Naciketas did not invent a ritual by himself. He received it through instruction. The action became meaningful because it was grounded in revealed knowledge.
This story connects to 3.15 because karma is born of the Veda. Yajña is not random action. It is known through śāstra and teacher. Naciketas receiving the fire teaching shows that sacred action depends on revealed knowledge, not personal imagination.

3. Bharadvāja Learns the Vastness of the Veda from Indra (Taittirīya Brāhmaṇa / Traditional Vedic Story)
Bharadvāja Ṛṣi spent his life studying the Veda with great dedication. He did not treat learning casually. His years were offered to śravaṇa, recitation, discipline, and preservation of sacred knowledge.
Even after long study, the Veda remained vast. The tension in the story is the limitation of human life before the immeasurable depth of Vedic wisdom. A person may be sincere and brilliant, but the Veda cannot be exhausted by human effort alone.
Indra appeared to Bharadvāja and showed him three great mountains, representing the vastness of the Veda. From each mountain, Indra took only a handful and showed it to the sage. What Bharadvāja had studied, even after such effort, was like those handfuls compared to the mountains.
Bharadvāja did not become discouraged. He understood the greatness of the Veda and the humility required before it. The story teaches reverence: the Veda is not a small human book to be mastered and dismissed; it is vast, sacred, and connected to divine wisdom.
This story connects to 3.15 because the verse says the Veda comes from akṣaram, Bhagavān. Bharadvāja’s experience shows that the Veda is not merely human literature. It is vast, sacred revelation from which karma, yajña, and dharma are known.

4. Yājñavalkya Receives the Śukla Yajurveda from Sūrya (Purāṇic / Vedic Tradition)
Yājñavalkya was a brilliant student, but a conflict arose with his teacher Vaiśampāyana. He was asked to return the Yajurveda he had learned. Yājñavalkya gave it back and stood without that knowledge.
But he did not abandon the pursuit. He turned to Sūrya with tapas and prayer. Through the grace of Sūrya, he received the Śukla Yajurveda, a fresh revelation of Vedic knowledge.
The tension is powerful: knowledge once possessed was lost; pride had to be humbled; tapas and devotion became necessary. Yājñavalkya did not manufacture the Veda through cleverness. He received it through divine grace.
The result was the transmission of the Śukla Yajurveda, which became a sacred branch of Vedic learning and ritual knowledge.
This story connects to 3.15 because the verse says the Veda is born of the Imperishable, not of human intellect. Even a brilliant sage like Yājñavalkya receives Vedic knowledge through divine source and grace. Sacred karma and yajña depend on that revealed Vedic wisdom.

5. Śaunaka Approaches Aṅgiras for Higher Knowledge (Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad)
Śaunaka was a great householder, respected and accomplished. He had wealth, status, and responsibility. But he knew that worldly success alone could not answer the deepest question of life.
He approached Sage Aṅgiras properly, with humility, and asked: “What is that, knowing which everything becomes known?” This was not casual curiosity. It was the turning of a mature person toward śāstra and teacher.
Aṅgiras then taught him by distinguishing lower and higher knowledge. The Vedas, phonetics, ritual, grammar, and other disciplines were taught as important forms of knowledge, but the highest knowledge is that by which the Imperishable is known.
The tension is between being satisfied with outer accomplishment and seeking the source of all wisdom. Śaunaka did not invent his own path. He approached the teacher in the Vedic way and received knowledge through sampradāya.
This story connects to 3.15 because the verse traces karma to Veda and Veda to akṣaram. Śaunaka’s question shows the mature attitude toward Vedic wisdom: learn from the proper source, understand action and knowledge in their place, and seek the Imperishable from which the Veda itself ultimately comes.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भवम् विद्धि ब्रह्म अक्षरसमुद्भवम् । तस्मात् सर्वगतम् ब्रह्म नित्यम् यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम् ॥३.१५॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: कर्म | (karma) = action, karma-yoga, prescribed action ब्रह्मोद्भवम् | (brahmodbhavam) = born of brahma, born of the Veda विद्धि | (viddhi) = know ब्रह्म | (brahma) = the Veda अक्षरसमुद्भवम् | (akṣarasamudbhavam) = born of the Imperishable Bhagavān तस्मात् | (tasmāt) = therefore सर्वगतम् | (sarvagatam) = all-pervading ब्रह्म | (brahma) = the Veda नित्यम् | (nityam) = always यज्ञे | (yajñe) = in yajña प्रतिष्ठितम् | (pratiṣṭhitam) = established
rich_grammar.anvayah: कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भवं विद्धि । ब्रह्म अक्षरसमुद्भवम् । तस्मात् सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम् ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: ब्रह्म + उद्भवम् = ब्रह्मोद्भवम् | ब्रह्म + अक्षरसमुद्भवम् = ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भवम् | तस्मात् + सर्वगतम् = तस्मात्सर्वगतम् | सर्वगतम् + ब्रह्म = सर्वगतं | नित्यम् + यज्ञे = नित्यं
rich_grammar.samasa: ब्रह्मोद्भवम् = ब्रह्मणः उद्भवः यस्य तत् Samāsa sense: बहुव्रीहि / षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष-like adjectival expression; that which is born of brahma, the Veda. | अक्षरसमुद्भवम् = अक्षरात् समुद्भवः यस्य तत् Samāsa sense: पञ्चमी-तत्पुरुष-like adjectival expression; that which arises from the Imperishable. | सर्वगतम् = सर्वं गतम् / सर्वत्र गतम् Samāsa sense: समासिक विशेषण; all-pervading.
rich_grammar.other: विद्धि is लोट्-लकारः, मध्यमपुरुष एकवचनम्, from विद्, meaning “know.” | कर्म is understood as the object of विद्धि through ब्रह्मोद्भवम्: “know karma to be born of the Veda.” | ब्रह्म is used in this verse in the sense of the Veda, not Brahman as pure consciousness in the primary Upaniṣadic sense. | अक्षरम् refers here to the Imperishable Bhagavān, the source of the Veda. | यज्ञे is सप्तमी विभक्ति: “in yajña.” | प्रतिष्ठितम् is a past passive participial form from प्रति + स्था, meaning “established.” | नित्यम् functions adverbially: “always.”

key_verses.final_takeaway: The Veda, which guides all karma and yajña, is born from the imperishable Brahman. Since Brahman pervades everything, the sacred cycle of giving and offering is eternally established and cannot be abandoned.
